Indeed, what a mess. That should work (also moved it in scope because I didn't see a reason for it to be out?):
```diff
diff --git a/modules/openxr/openxr_action_sets.cpp b/modules/openxr/openxr_action_sets.cpp
index 9d6e23ae56..867f5547cf 100644
--- a/modules/openxr/openxr_action_sets.cpp
+++ b/modules/openxr/openxr_action_sets.cpp
@@ -152,9 +152,10 @@ void OpenXRActionSets::create_default_action_sets() {
 	add_action_set(action_set);
 
 	// Create our actions
+	// clang-format off
 #define add_action(varname, actionname, actiontype, toplevel_paths) \
-	Ref<OpenXRAction>##varname;                                     \
 	{                                                               \
+		Ref<OpenXRAction> ##varname;                                \
 		##varname.instantiate();                                    \
 		##varname->set_name(String(#varname));                      \
 		##varname->set_localised_name(String(actionname));          \
@@ -162,6 +163,7 @@ void OpenXRActionSets::create_default_action_sets() {
 		##varname->parse_toplevel_paths(String(toplevel_paths));    \
 		action_set->add_action(##varname);                          \
 	}
+	// clang-format on
 
 	add_action(trigger, "Trigger", OpenXRAction::OPENXR_ACTION_FLOAT, "/user/hand/left,/user/hand/right");
 	add_action(trigger_click, "Trigger Click", OpenXRAction::OPENXR_ACTION_BOOL, "/user/hand/left,/user/hand/right");
```

`clang-format` is only a formatting tool, it can't change code.

`clang-tidy` can do code analysis and changes, including giving a default initialization to uninitialized variables, though it can be somewhat unwieldy to use (applies to the whole case, has false positives and creates compilation bugs).

body:
My main reason for not doing this is that the implementation is purely based on OpenXR, there really isn't a way to abstract things to some sort of template class that makes a lot of sense and especially for the vulkan extension, we'd be implementing far more on the dummy class while it is never even instantiated (the whole point is that if OpenXR is not available or not running, there will never be an instance).

This really is uniquely OpenXR and frankly frankenstein enough, while I understand why OpenXR went down this path, I really don't think they've architectures this very nicely. From a principled view they way OpenXR sets its hooks into Vulkan only makes sense for a purpose build XR engine.

author: akien-mga

body:
```suggestion
				Returns the 2D analog of the cross product for this vector and [code]with[/code].
				This is the signed area of the parallelogram formed by the two vectors. If the second vector is clockwise from the first vector, then the cross product is the positive area. If counter-clockwise, the cross product is the negative area.
```

I'm not sure that the `[b]Note:[/b]` part is helpful. Do Godot users care about how something is originally defined by mathematicians for usage in 3D, or do they care about how it's defined in 2D and in Godot? We are already saying that Vector2's cross method is the closest analog to the cross product.

The cross product in 2D is an extension of the original 3D definition, losing the property of the resulting vector being perpendicular to both input vectors (which is impossible in 2D unless the two vectors are colinear but then the cross product is zero anyway) (plus the result isn't a vector). It's the closest analog that exists.

We could keep both explanations if it's helpful for people to understand.

We could also note for Vector3 that the magnitude of the cross product vector is the area of the parallelogram formed by the two vectors. For 3D, we don't have to worry about signed area because that's just indicated by which direction the output vector points.

body: Should it be `CMP_EPSILON` or `CMP_EPSILON2`
author: AThousandShips

body:
Ah yes, I'm expecting some discussion on the epsilon, and will need a bit of testing. I saw `CMP_EPSILON2` but wanted to make sure it is ok for this use with 32 bit `real_t`. But you are correct we are using squares here.

It has to be a value that is large enough that when `sqrt`ed afterwards, it still provides accuracy when used as the divisor. So I think a bit of testing will be good to check this in practice rather than going straight for the smaller `CMP_EPSILON2` (without knowing how it was tested).
author: lawnjelly

body: I'm fully agnostic and the only difference with 64 bit would be the lower limit on magnitude
author: AThousandShips

body:
32 / 64 bit epsilon differences can be quite difficult.

Last time we discussed it (that I remember), reduz was in favour of keeping the 32 bit epsilons when compiling as 64 bit. This loses out on some potential accuracy in 64 bit, but if you use different epsilons you open up a massive surface of difficult to find bugs. Epsilon bugs are some of the _worst_ imo. :grin: 

Anyway I'm doing some empirical testing on the epsilon as we speak. :+1: 
Ogre looks like it uses `1e-8f`, which is larger than `CMP_EPSILON2` (which is `1e-10f`).
author: lawnjelly

body:
> Epsilon bugs are some of the worst imo.

Agreed
author: AThousandShips

body:
Ok after (I hope) some testing, I'm satisfied that `CMP_EPSILON2` should be okay at 32 bit, so I've pushed a version with this epsilon.

I tested in gdscript then in a c++ testbed. Of course there's always the chance I messed up, it's pretty easy to mess up, with math precision. :grin:
author: lawnjelly

body: This would be the only method exposed to GDScript that mutates the struct. That's a bit odd. Is this method necessary to expose, or is it intended to be an internal helper for the C++ code?
author: aaronfranke

body:
There are some issues with it because of access issues with GDScript, primitive types are non-mutable when returned, including as properties, GDScript passes them by copy not reference, unless that has changed, as in mutating functions cannot affect them

See #70995
author: AThousandShips

body:
If you can think of an easy way to return two values efficiently from bound functions, I'm happy to modify the PR.

If we don't want it to mutate the struct, we need to return the new Vector _and_ the length (or the new Vector _and_ success or failure).

Originally this was non-bound, but it is now also a potential candidate solution to the problem in #74852, and whichever solution we go with needs to be available from script.

Another non-mutating alternative would be a `normalized()` function that returns either the normalized vector or failure (maybe a Vector or NULL? can that work?).
author: lawnjelly

body:
Perhaps then for binding returning a variant that is either the normalized value or NULL is the way to go `try_normalized()` or something like that?

I can modify this particular PR to have this function unbound as before, but will be good to get some discussion going on how to solve the gdscript problem.

body: I don't understand why this would be needed. If the script is loaded by the resource loader it should already be fully compiled.
author: vnen

body:
This is what exactly happens in the reproduction project. Before reaching this part of the code (in _parse_expression > case GDScriptParser::Node::IDENTIFIER), it will go first to GDScriptCompiler::_gdtype_from_datatype > GDScriptCache::get_shallow_script, where it calls `script->set_path(p_path, true);` which push the uncompiled script inside the resource loader.
Another working solution is to change `get_shallow_script` to `get_full_script`, but it might have performance impact.
author: Blackiris

body:
Ah, I just didn't realize that `Resource::set_path()` was changing the cache, I wrongly assumed it only changed a property. So the proper fix would be to move this call from `get_shallow_script()` to `get_full_script()`.

Then there's also the issue with builtin scripts, since they don't really have a path, so there needs to be a way to index them in the GDScript cache so the `finish_compiling()` call can properly reference them.

This issue isn't simple to solve.
author: vnen

sorry, I wasn't suggesting changing the macro, just including it to show it doesn't have the necessary 'zero if zero' branch.

This is the adapted algorithm used in my PR:
http://terathon.com/code/oblique.html

Specifically, he uses an inline function:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/40372043/148779041-6732cf42-091d-4d32-abd9-8d0470f1b012.png)

which you can see does greatly simplify the expression:

author: qaptoR

body:
In master, it's:

```cpp
#ifndef SIGN
#define SIGN(m_v) (((m_v) == 0) ? (0.0) : (((m_v) < 0) ? (-1.0) : (+1.0)))
#endif
```

We could backport `SIGN` with the behavior of `0.0` returning `0.0` and keep `SGN` the way it is.
author: aaronfranke

body:
That seems like a reasonable option, especially given that according to Wikipedia, the signum (aka sign) function should return zero if zero

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/40372043/148807921-72f9eb5a-e079-46be-9256-a3de996d91ec.png)

There's also this answer from stackoverflow, which might be worth looking into as an optimization: https://stackoverflow.com/a/4609795/11622959
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/40372043/148808309-9893f700-2107-4c77-b63e-83613ac81813.png)
author: qaptoR

body:
I think the real question is "is there a distinct difference"? any mathematicians kicking around?

Looking around I found this collection of articles. I mention it not just because of their crazy bit-twiddling, but because it shows there are legitimately multiple types of sign-checking, hence it makes sense why SGN does not currently return zero. It probably wasn't needed more generally.

http://graphics.stanford.edu/~seander/bithacks.html#:~:text=your%20target%20architecture.-,Compute%20the%20sign%20of%20an%20integer,-int%20v%3B%20%20%20%20%20%20//%20we

body: Importantly, cubic interpolation avoids discontinuities in the 2nd order derivative of the noise function. This is important when using noise as a bumpmap. Regular value noise will show sharp discontinuities when used as a normal map that cubic interpolation avoids
author: clayjohn

body: An important distinguishing feature of simplex noise over other gradient noise types is that the gradients exist in a simplex lattice rather than a grid lattice. This helps avoid directional artifacts that perlin noise suffers from. It also means that it scales better with an increase in dimensions (once you hit 4 or more dimensions simplex noise becomes much faster than perlin)
author: clayjohn

body:
We had a lengthy discussion about this in today's PR review meeting. The consensus was to merge this for now and then evaluate user feedback during the alphas and betas. With the new noise abstract class, it seems like FastNoise would be a great fit for a first-class GDExtension, but the GDExtension system is still relatively new, and we want to get this in the engine and get user feedback now.

Depending on user feedback, (i.e. if users find the current options too complicated) we are considering exposing a simpler type of noise only by default and then exposing the full range of noise types and parameters in an official GDExtension. 

To merge this, the documentation needs to be finished.

One other consideration is ``get_noise_4d()`` I don't like taking away a function that was available in 3.x. Is there any way that we can implement something on top of FastNoise that exposes ``get_noise_4d()``?
author: clayjohn

author: clayjohn

body:
None of the `get_noise` functions are constant, that looks like an oversight. They can be, see my implementation https://github.com/Zylann/godot_voxel/blob/4c3777ce00473efb426362b83d3c3d5853a2ced0/util/noise/fast_noise_lite.h#L101
Same for `get_image` functions
author: Zylann

body: Does pure virtual means extensions cannot define new noises?
author: Zylann

body:
`virtual` means referencing a `Noise*` that points to a `FastNoiseLite` object will call the FastNoiseLite implementation in the derived class rather than the implementation in `Noise` without casting.

`= 0` means there is no implementation, thus one cannot instantiate Noise().

I don't know about GDExtensions, but when I wrote this as a module this was exactly so I could define OpenSimplexNoise, FastNoise, and FastNoiseSIMD objects, and an unknown number of other types in the future.
author: TokisanGames

body:
What was the idea behind converting this and all of these functions to a template? What other types will be used here? This alpha_blend function is specifically designed for a 32-bit long int, the `l` component of the l2c union. I think there will be unexpected results with other types.

Are there any active use cases right now for generating non-32-bit noise images? Were they tested and do they work, including with the alpha blend seamless function?
author: TokisanGames

body:
I think the purpose of this was to allow for grayscale image optimizations when a noise generator creates an image with `Image::FORMAT_L8` (see the template specialization and the get_seamless_image implementation in noise.cpp). 
But looking at the code now I think there is room for improvement, which I might include in a subsequent PR. E.g. I can't remember why I didn't just overload the alpha blend function.
author: Geometror

body: I just noticed that the functions should actually bound with GDVIRTUAL_BIND(), but I am not exactly sure.
author: Geometror

body: You're right, for FastNoiseLite they could be `const`. But then the interface methods would need to be `const` too and I am not sure if we should change it (since someone might want to add a noise generator which uses some cache structure internally or something else which requires write access in the get_noise/get_image functions). Or should we prohibit that as it might lead to bad library/API design?
author: Geometror

body:
It sounds like bad design to me. I care about this in particular because I use one of these in a multithreaded generator, and seeing a non-const call to a noise class from multiple threads is a bit worrying^^"
If someone reaaaally wants to mutate something they can use `mutable`, though for Noise algorithms specifically I don't see a good reason (or it's not noise anymore).
author: Zylann

body: Why isn't it just called `gradient`?
author: aaronfranke

body: I dont know if that's related since I didnt implement this PR, but when speaking of noise, "gradient" can also relate to the derivative of noise (which in practical terms gives normals, or displacement vectors instead of a single value). This property is for the very specific use case of generating a colored image with a fixed conversion formula used exclusively in the `get_image` function. Maybe it could be called `color_gradient`?
author: Zylann

body:
Ramp is the term used in industry standard VFX tools like Adobe AfterFX and Houdini, even Blender. 

Gradient is a type of noise. 
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gradient_noise 

Godot is the oddball by calling color ramps gradients.
author: TokisanGames

body: Interesting. Godot 2 used the name ColorRamp, but in Godot 3 it was renamed to Gradient. If it goes against the rest of the industry then that seems like a strange change, and I can see how it's confusing in the context of Gradient noise, but without this context it seems like a good rename (Gradient sounds better to me).
author: aaronfranke

body: As Zylann suggested, I think `color_gradient` (or in this specific case `modulation_color_gradient`) would be fine, since it sets it apart enaugh from a gradient in the context of noise generation.

body:
<details><summary> Bad suggestion, nothing to see here :sweat_smile: </summary>
I believe the idea is right, however with a further look at the parameters of `_parse_expression`, it looks like the `true` here isn't completely random and was probably intended for `p_initializer`, which is used for ignoring locals. See:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/2f4d76f068b29783bde653406b51909b29a082a3/modules/gdscript/gdscript_compiler.cpp#L1988

So I believe it should be:

```suggestion
				GDScriptCodeGenerator::Address src_addr = _parse_expression(codegen, r_error, parameter->default_value, false, true);
```
</details>
author: cdemirer

body:
I think my earlier suggestion would actually break this:
```gdscript
func f(a, b=a): prints(a, b)
```
So just removing the `true` is probably the best (`false` is already the default).
```suggestion
				GDScriptCodeGenerator::Address src_addr = _parse_expression(codegen, r_error, parameter->default_value);
```
author: cdemirer

body:
I thought having `false` there explicitly might be clearer, but sure.

Is the ability to refer to "earlier" parameters intended? C++ does not allow that. If so, this should probably also be documented.
BTW, I think setting initializer to true would also break referring to other local variables.

When I get back to my machine, I will try to add some clarifying tests regarding that.

body: Interesting, is there an implicit conversion from `Ref<T>` to `String` for use in `vformat`? I used to call `->to_string()` manually.
author: akien-mga

body: Nitpick but this could come first to avoid the while loop in this no-op case.
author: akien-mga

body:
Actually I haven't even thought of that, just wrote it like this and it worked. :smile: 

Just looked at it and `vformat` seems to take in `const Variant &` and there's a `operator Variant()` in `Ref<T>`. And `vformat` passes such `Variant` to `String::sprintf` which, in case of `%s`, (indirectly) calls `Variant::operator String()` which calls `Variant::stringify`. Something like that.

Modules can be disabled, so this will break the build for `module_gridmap_enabled=no`. So it needs checks:
```suggestion

#include "modules/modules_enabled.gen.h" # For gridmap.
#ifdef MODULE_GRIDMAP_ENABLED
#include "modules/gridmap/grid_map.h"
#endif
```
And same `#ifdef`s where `GridMap` is actually used.

The added dependency on a module somewhat breaks encapsulation, but it's not like it's without precedent in the engine so if there's no way to do this without special casing `GridMap` here, I guess we'll have to live with it.

body:
The original was better actually:
```suggestion
			_set_error(vformat(RTR("Invalid number of arguments when calling stage function '%s', which expects %d arguments."), String(name), sf.arguments.size()));
```
"An" could be added but then it calls for a more complete sentence, like "An invalid number of arguments **was passed** when calling stage function '%s'...". Without it, it's just a nominal group, which is a common construct for error messages to avoid having to repeat the same verbs over and over (like "Got an invalid number of ....").

body:
```suggestion
				_set_error(vformat(RTR("A switch may only contain '%s' and '%s' blocks."), "case", "default"));
```
For the record, when a translated string is used only once and the substitutions are constants, usually it would be best not to use substitutions as it's easier for translators to understand the context and how to translate. But here using substitutions helps prevent translators from wrongly translating `case` and `default` in their language, so that makes sense :+1:

body:

```suggestion
							_set_error(RTR("Array initialization is only supported on high-end platforms."));
```
Just as a tip (so I'm no English expert), using plural is a common trick to avoid having to use an indefinite article which could sound weird ("a high-end platform" - which one? With plurals it's clear that it's any high-end platform, even if there might only be one supported).
author: akien-mga

body:
This is tricky for translation, there are good chances that translators might remove the spaces by mistake, and it's not sure that `<arg0> and <arg1> and <arg2>` is something that can be translated in the same order for any language (for example some languages might have constructs like `<arg0> <arg1>-and <arg2>-and`? Not that I know any like this but typically anything we can think of might exist somewhere :)).

So this might be worth refactoring a bit. Ideally there shouldn't be any English text in that `at` I think. Maybe just:
```suggestion
							at += ", ";
```

body: What does this change do?
author: akien-mga

body:
So how it worked was that you get two lists, just own properties and all the properties including parent classes. Both are reported in their bind order, similar to inspector. Then we iterate the list of all properties. The assumption is that the list of all properties contains all the own properties first. So by the time `EO` is null, the own properties have ended and it is safe to assume every other property is inherited.

While `EO` is true we compare the values (below) and move the cursor one position forward. This still works when I add sorting, but the caveat is that we can no longer assume that own properties are the first ones. They still follow the same order, just have gaps of inherited properties in between. So we can no longer use that old default value, but it's safe to default to `true` instead. Every own property will be caught by the check below, as long as there is anything left to check.

author: clayjohn

body:
I was having unexplained corruption with some texture dimensions and traced it to these returned width/height values (returned by `Image::get_image_mipmap_offset_and_dimensions`).

For example, on the 12x12 texture (block=4), I was seeing this. First loop iteration
```
{
  bw=12, bh=12
  s=144
  w = 6, h = 6
  *r_mm_width = (12>>1) = 6
  *r_mm_height = (12>>1) = 6
}
```
Second loop iteration
```
{
  bw=8, bh=8
  s=64
  w = 3, h = 3
  *r_mm_width = (8>>1) = 4 <-- wrong!
  *r_mm_height = (8>>1) = 4 <-- wrong!
}
```
Third loop iteration
```
{
  bw=4, bh=4
  s=16
  w = 1, h = 1
  *r_mm_width = (4>>1) = 2 <-- wrong!
  *r_mm_height = (4>>1) = 2 <-- wrong!
}
```

Note that the behavior of this function was altered slightly by 81ed9fa31bc63823235f64c88d441a8e530ab039
However, It was also wrong before. It's possible either that Godot always had this bug with 2x2 and 1x1 mipmaps reading out-of-bounds, or didn't cause an issue before because the old `etc` compression library ignored the out-of-bounds data.

I can't see any reason for the return values to use these "half-block" values. I would think if I want to know the dimensions of a mipmap level, I would want the actual dimensions, namely `w` and `h`. I can't find any callers of this code which would be adversely affected by this change.

In case you want to check my assertions, the only public functions using these `*r_mm_` return values are `Image::get_image_mipmap_size` and `Image::get_image_mipmap_offset_and_dimensions`.

I did some further digging into that logic, so a quick history breakdown. @reduz originally added this `*r_mm_width = bw;` statement in 0586e184490fd132f99acb1a67c788959cfdbade . This function was only used in one place, in what is now `servers/rendering/renderer_rd/renderer_storage_rd.cpp`. However, the function was only ever used with FORMAT_RGBA8 for the longest time, meaning block size is 1 and bw == w and bh == h. For this reason, I suspect reduz either never tested it with compressed formats, or had a different intended usecase in mind than what it has ultimately been used for.

Alright, I know I talk too much. I hope this answers your question about why this code change was necessary, why it was relevant to this PR, and why it evaded notice for so long. I'll also loop in @floppyhammer as they made the more recent commit here.
author: lyuma

The block size matching logic was there before that PR. As I haven't got a Vulkan-capable computer on hand right now, I will try to take a closer look at this on Monday.

body:
The check is correct but since it's always true (Godot can't compile without C++17 or later, even if changed manually in `SConstruct`), we can simplify further:
```suggestion
#ifndef _NO_DISCARD_
#define _NO_DISCARD_ [[nodiscard]]
#endif
```

I agree about keeping the macro itself for now so that relevant changes are easy to cherry-pick to `3.x`.

body:
No, and @akien-mga did get me to remove the pre - c++ 17 defines in the Godot 4 version, so it is an option to remove this part.

However personally I would be tempted to err on the side of leaving this for forward compatibility - should we decide in future to compile for c++ 17, it should just work (TM) even better in the same way as 4.x, and no need to revisit the code (and it is fresh in mind at the moment, but may not be in e.g. a years time).
author: lawnjelly

body: Yeah it's good to have it included if we ever decide to move to C++17 in `3.x`, then it will just work (and we can eventually cleanup the unneeded non-standard attributes).
author: akien-mga

body: Also useful in the year 2050 when you want to compile 3.5, and none of the compilers still have extensions from the stone age. (This is a big thing apparently, the tool chain getting outdated rather than the code).
author: lawnjelly

body:
This message is worded with "may" as it's not possible to check whether the headers have been sent correctly – only whether cross-origin isolation is enabled (which itself depends on a secure context). The browser also does not inform us *why* cross-origin isolation is disabled, so we can only do guesswork here.

On the other hand, we can check whether the browser thinks the current page is a secure context (and that's what I did above to conditionally display that reason message).

The code looks just about the same as the GDScript implementation, except for the missing `Trim()`. Also needs a few code style fixes.

However, I'm not sure if using internal calls would be preferred here since otherwise we need to make sure it's kept in sync.
author: raulsntos

body:
In GDScript's implementation the `to_lower()` call is removed (since `camel_to_underscore(true)` already converts to lowercase) and a call to `strip_edges()` is added at the end which is equivalent to C#'s `Trim()`. So we should do that too for consistency.

```suggestion
            string aux = instance.CamelcaseToUnderscore(true).Replace("_", " ").Trim();
```
author: raulsntos

body:
This does not seem like the right fix. Usually, when an edited object is modified, the inspector automatically detects the changes done to a modified property. Thus the change of the `path` property should be detected by the Inspector, and it should automatically trigger an update.

As a consequence, the change should likely be made in the InspectorDock, not here.

Edit: this automatic detection is only on master. For 3.x the change is detected by listening to the `"changed"`signal, emitted by `Resource::emit_changed()`

author: rafallus

body:
That might work, though I'm surprised that there wouldn't be an existing system that can be suitable for GridMap. It's not the only node / editor that handles picking and if other gizmos can do it, then GridMap should be able to do it too (just talking in principle - I'm not actually familiar with that code and might be wrong).

CC @JFonS @RandomShaper
author: akien-mga

body: Well, that can certainly be done with the normal `unhandled_input`. But since the viewport is already checking for collisions I think that can be used and avoid repeating code. Also, the `process_picking` method (the one that takes care of collisions) is called inside `physics_process` of the SceneTree. If we want the input to also be processed in GridMap's `physics_process`, the inputs should be collected first and then processed. To me, that's a bit too much for something that is already implemented and can be reused.

body:
This description is true but it might be not descriptive enough as:

>relative to the origin of the [CanvasLayer] that this [CanvasItem] is in

doesn't state in what way it's relative to the origin (origin is just a point). It may be not obvious that the returned position is described in the given `CanvasLayer`'s basis vectors. Meaning if that `CanvasLayer` is anyhow rotated, scaled, skewed, etc. (in other words when its basis is not standard) then there still might be some confusion about the result.

A description like:

>Returns the mouse's position in the coordinate space of the [CanvasLayer] that this [CanvasItem] is in.

doesn't have this issue (at least I think so) but it's probably not user-friendly as there are many no-math users (_"what is coordinate space?!"_). Maybe such description would be good if "coordinate space" would link to some page describing what it means? :thinking: 

My point is that current descriptions (the same issue with the other similar changes) are not fully descriptive. There's still missing information (relative in what way?) in them. I can't find wording to be both descriptive ("in the coordinate space") and user-friendly ("relative to") in a compact way. Maybe you could come up with something like that.

@kleonc I've updated it to:
```Returns the mouse's position in the [CanvasLayer] that this [CanvasItem] is in using the coordinate system of the [CanvasLayer].```


>  Maybe such description would be good if "coordinate space" would link to some page describing what it means?

There is a tutorial page on [Matrices and transforms](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/tutorials/math/matrices_and_transforms.html), but I'm not convinced it's helpful to link each method to this page.

author: KoBeWi

body: It's not enough, for example if `texdraw == normal` (before the first changed check) and `normal == focused` and the button is focused then `normal` texture won't be drawn. And even though in such case `focused` texture will be drawn below anyway (and it's the same as `normal`) it won't be drawn e.g. with tiling applied appropriately. Meaning check in this line should fail in such case but it wouldn't.
author: kleonc

body:
> It's not enough, for example if texdraw == normal (before the first changed check) and normal == focused and the button is focused then normal texture won't be drawn.

Yes, that's the issue this PR fixes. But what I said (which might not be obviously visible in the diff) is that `(texdraw == focused)` should be changed to `(draw_focus && texdraw == focused)`, so the "do nothing" code is only executed when the focus is supposed to be drawn (`draw_focus`). I checked and it fixes the issue, while not re-introducing the previous bug.

Although I wonder why I didn't do it like that in my original PR; I remember considering it at least.
author: KoBeWi

body:
> But what I said (which might not be obviously visible in the diff) is that `(texdraw == focused)` should be changed to `(draw_focus && texdraw == focused)`

Yeah, I've got what you've suggested after the first comment of yours.

>so the "do nothing" code is only executed when the focus is supposed to be drawn (`draw_focus`). I checked and it fixes the issue, while not re-introducing the previous bug.

The thing is with your approach that texture will be drawn just once in the "draw `focused` texture" part of code. And that's incorrect, it should also be drawn in "draw `texdraw` texture` part of code.

Example with repeatable texture and button in tile + expand mode:

- Button unfocused:
![Godot_v3 4 2-stable_win64_5ikwt6fn8N](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9283098/151679877-1c98fcfb-fefe-4177-b1ee-9db270087478.png)

- Button focused:
  - `3.4.2.stable` / `3.x` with my fix:

body: A couple of lines down, a call to `mesh_surface_get_lod()` is called which accepts `distance * lod_distance_multiplier` which then divides some values by this to get effective screen size. `0.0` would cause the highest LOD to always be shown and `distance_min` would still take into account the distance between the camera and the mesh which shouldn't happen in an orthogonal camera. `1.0` would then just effectively pass in the `lod_distance_multiplier` itself which is the size of an orthogonal camera or the fov of a perspective camera thus removing any effect distance would have.

body:
Technically, we should be using the same formula as with shadow sizes above the default. However, this would result in too much peter-panning, so I went for a half-way compromise between not adjusting the bias at all and adjusting it to match the resolution perfectly.

The bias factors used for each resolution are:

- 256: `8.5`
- 512: `4.5`
- 1024: `2.5`
- 2048: `1.5`
- 4096: `1.0`
- 8192: `0.5`
- 16384: `0.25`

This applies to both directional and point light shadows.
author: Calinou

body:
This logic should be different for Point Light Shadows, as you have to have the resolution of the cell the shadow is using, not the entire shadow map.

This would incorrectly change bias for the same effective resolution of shadow if you go from a 4k shadow map with 4 4 4 4 quadrants and change to 8k shadow map resolution with 16 16 16 16 quadrants which effectively results in same resolution, just more shadows available.

body:
I know this is already merged but I decided to look over it anyway and I give it a ✅

Everything in this PR falls into one of these 3 categories:

* The float literal has an identical value to the double literal (such as sums of powers of two `0.5f` or integers `0.0f`, `1.0f`, etc)
* The value is something we don't need the exact value of (such as checking if a value is `> 0.9999f`)
* The value is in Color, which should be always 32-bit anyway (since more precision is useless for Color)

body:
There seems to be a inconsistency between the Gradle and SCons targets.

"Debug" template *are* built with `scons tools=no target=release_debug`. The `target=debug` platform is only used for actual engine debugging (i.e. getting dev asserts, more information on crashes, etc.), and we don't ship any binaries built with `target=debug` (this is only for custom builds for people who know they need it).

I see that `platform/android/detect.py` doesn't seem to do anything special for `target=release_debug` compared to `target=release`, which is a discrepancy with all other platforms (note that it's `startswith`, applies to both `release` and `release_debug`): https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/f5b9cbaff6f0a058d08187b3124948ae68848cd0/platform/android/detect.py#L189
author: akien-mga

body:
That doesn't match the build matrix for other platforms.

The official released (production) editor binaries use `target=release_debug`. The editor can't be used with `target=release` only, as debugging tools are then not included, and it would crash on errors instead of reporting them.

So the only two editor targets you should support are:
`tools=yes target=release_debug` -> production build, official releases
`tools=yes target=debug` -> development build, used by engine developers (slow and not meant for use in production)

body:
> I see that `platform/android/detect.py` doesn't seem to do anything special for `target=release_debug` compared to `target=release`, which is a discrepancy with all other platforms (note that it's `startswith`, applies to both `release` and `release_debug`):

Actually that part seems to be intentional, the difference is made in `SConstruct` directly for all platforms, seems like we factored this out at some point. Other platforms like Linux still make a difference between the two in their `detect.py` but it's just to switch from `-O3` to `-O2` between `release` and `release_debug` (and on Android we seem to only use `-O2` so it's not needed for now).

body:
Tested on a Xiaomi Pocophone F1, it seems to work great! No issue building the APK from Linux either with the provided instructions.
I could install the 2D platformer from the Asset Library and run it, with touchscreen controls already supported (that's luck, it's one of the few demos that supports it :P).

Seems good to merge, and we can iterate further in new issues/PRs.

Some ideas/remarks for future work:
- It would be good if the Back button when running a project from the editor could close the project and go back to the editor (currently it closes everything). It's already handled in the editor itself to prevent it from closing the editor, but not at runtime.
- We often have pre-selected filter text fields in the UI, and on Android (at least without a bluetooth keyboard) that shows the virtual keyboard. It confirms that the virtual keyboard works well and is properly called when in a LineEdit, but here we might want to figure out a way to prevent it from showing automatically on such pre-selected fields, and only show it when the user actually clicks the field.

Ah that default should be fixed IMO, I would expect it to still default to builtin templates.

```
def get_flags():

```
which changes the default value for `tools` (but it can still be overridden with `tools=yes`).

So I guess the problem happens at the gradle step?
author: akien-mga

body:
Okay. So what's changed then is that the default target "debug" now builds a `dev.aar` instead of a `debug.aar`.

FYI: Previously I was using the following to build a quick test debug export template for android x86 emulators:
```
scons -j8 platform=android android_arch=x86
```
And then I run `./gradlew generateDevTemplate`
I now need to use:
```
scons -j8 target=release_debug platform=android android_arch=x86
```

Which matches the documentation, but seems less intuitive. I would expect `release_debug` to be for the editor builds  i.e. with `tools=yes`. If we're changing behaviour, it may be a good opportunity to change the names to match outputs and expectations.
author: madmiraal

body:
For `generateDevTemplate` is should be using `target=debug`, this might be a bug. `target=debug` is only used for development purposes and doesn't match the "Debug Template" that we build with `target=release_debug` (and which is the one `generateGodotTemplates` should use).

So it might have been an oversight among the other buildsystem changes.

> If we're changing behaviour, it may be a good opportunity to change the names to match outputs and expectations.

For `3.x` I think we should aim not to change behavior. For `master` I plan to implement https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/3371 which should help clean this up for Android too.

body:
```suggestion
    if os.access("bin/godot" + suffix, os.F_OK) and not os.access("bin/godot" + suffix, os.W_OK):
        suffixes = suffix.split(".")  # Split suffix string so we can add suffix number later
        extension = suffixes.pop()  # Store last suffix, should be the file extension.

        old_suffix = suffix  # Store old suffix to use in print statement
        suffix = ""

        MAXIMUM_ATTEMPTS = 10
        for suffix_number in range(1, MAXIMUM_ATTEMPTS + 1):
            new_suffix = ".".join(suffixes) + "." + str(suffix_number) + "." + extension
            if not os.access("bin/godot" + new_suffix, os.F_OK) or os.access(
                "bin/godot" + new_suffix, os.W_OK
            ):  # Check if this path can be written to
                suffix = new_suffix
                break

        if not suffix:
            print(
                "'bin/godot"
                + old_suffix
                + "' cannot be written to at this time. This can be caused when trying to compile to an executable that is currently open. Close Godot and try again."
            )
        else:
            print(
                "'bin/godot"
                + old_suffix
                + "' cannot be written to at this time. Compiling to 'bin/godot"
                + suffix
                + "' instead."
            )
    return suffix
```

```suggestion
        name, _, extension = suffix.rpartition(".")  # Split suffix string so we can add suffix number later
```

example of `rpartition()` below. The `name` will be returned as string, so no need to do `'.'.join(suffixes)` later again
```
>>> 'godot'.rpartition('.')
('', '', 'godot')

>>> 'foo.bar.godot'.rpartition('.')
('foo.bar', '.', 'godot')
```

body: The occlusion buffer size is not fixed, it depends on the "rendering/occlusion_culling/occlusion_rays_per_thread" project setting (I now see this may not be a very explicit setting name, we could change it). The resolution is chosen so the number of pixels in the occlusion buffer is approximately (rays_per_thread * cpu_physical_threads). This means CPUs with fewer cores will use a lower resolution, in an attempt to keep performance costs more even across devices.
author: JFonS

body:
```suggestion
		[PolygonOccluder3D] stores a polygon shape that can be used by the engine's occlusion culling system. When an [OccluderInstance3D] with a [PolygonOccluder3D] is selected in the editor, an editor will appear at the top of the 3D viewport so you can add/remove points. All points must be placed on the same 2D plane, which means it is not possible to create arbitrary 3D shapes with a single [PolygonOccluder3D]. To use arbitrary 3D shapes as occluders, use [ArrayOccluder3D] or [OccluderInstance3D]'s baking feature instead.
```

Could there be a better name for this? It strikes me as confusing.

In a sense with a window too, it is still the parent. Because the window is similar to a parent, and if the window isn't a direct parent, then the effects of the window are mediated by the direct parent?

```
IF MY PARENT IS VISIBLE IN THE TREE and I'M VISIBLE then I'M VISIBLE IN THE TREE
```

I'm not sure about antecedent - it is not really a commonly used English word, and most people would have to look it up to find the definition (including me as a native speaker :grinning: ).

You could also use `parent_and_window_visible_in_tree` potentially if worried about the window thing (not so relevant in 3.x but no problem having this carryover). Ancestors is fine too, to me it's a bit overkill because if your parent is visible in the tree, then by definition the ancestors are also visible.

But yeah ancestors is fine if you are struggling and don't want to use parent.

They do say naming things is one of the most difficult problems in computer science lol.

body:
It's hard to say for sure without digging around in Godbolt, but I think the compiler can optimize this more if we group together the `p_weight` multiplications:

```suggestion
						(2.0f * p_pre - 5.0f * p_from + 4.0f * p_to - p_post) * (p_weight * p_weight) +
						(-p_pre + 3.0f * p_from - 3.0f * p_to + p_post) * (p_weight * p_weight * p_weight));
```

An alternative implementation idea would be to wrap each step further down in parenthesis such that each step only uses one additional multiplication by `p_weight`. I'm not sure which is faster.

No, this is not a character count, but a glyph count, e.g., depending on font `Ē` might be two glyphs `E` and macron, and `ffi` might be one ligature glyph.

`main->lines[i]` have a `char_offset` and `char_count` fields you can use.
You can get a range of characters on each auto-wrapped line (relative to the line) using:
```c++
Vector2i range = TS->shaped_text_get_range(main->lines[i].text_buf->get_line_rid(j));
```

author: bruvzg

body:
I don't think this is the right solution. It seems like any time that `bisection_page == before_page_inclusive` automatically becomes a failure case because the method is called again with `bisection_page + 1` as `after_page_inclusive` which means the base case of `before_page_inclusive == after_page_inclusive` is never triggered because in that scope, `before_page_inclusive` is greater than `after_page_inclusive`. Satisfying that constraint is impossible. Could we try making the base case `before_page_inclusive >= after_page_inclusive` instead of using `==`?

If we do that though, it's not immediately clear to me whether we should return `before_page_inclusive` or `after_page_inclusive` in the base case. It could cause problems if we return the wrong one, because it could possibly be an empty page. Although, an empty page isn't the end of the world because playback should handle those gracefully.

I'm really sorry for how confusing this code is. My head is spinning trying to understand it now, months later.
author: ellenhp

body: Another option is modifying the loop to maybe be `test_page < before_page_inclusive` instead of `test_page <= before_page_inclusive`? I haven't tested either of these ideas but they seem like where I'd start. Thank you so much for figuring out where the problem is, by the way.
author: ellenhp

body:
In the MRP, it's kind of like searching for pos 0 inside index range `[0, 2]`:

| idx | pos | size |
|------|------|-------|
| 0     | 0     | 1     |
| 1     | 0     | 0     |
| 2     | 0     | 1     |

In this case, `bisection_page` first picks 2, and reset it to -1, and then searches forward and picks 0.

But yeah, it might fail in other cases :(

body:
It's likely that somewhere in the engine the value can get truncated to 32-bit floats. Having a stricter epsilon can be useful for debugging, but I wouldn't enable it by default.

```suggestion
#ifdef PRECISE_MATH_CHECKS
		return abs(s) < CMP_EPSILON_64;
#else
		return abs(s) < CMP_EPSILON;
#endif // PRECISE_MATH_CHECKS
```
author: aaronfranke

body:
The ideal epsilon for 64-bit floats is not the same as the epsilon for 32-bit floats squared. It's about `1e-14` or `0.00000000000001`.

Also, the current epsilon value for 32-bit floats could theoretically have an extra zero added since the ideal is about `1e-6`, but it's a risky change to do if something depends on the extra leniency. I'm not sure that I would bother defining `CMP_EPSILON_32` if it's just the same as `CMP_EPSILON`, but maybe this is a good opportunity to introduce a stricter epsilon value for 32-bit floats.

```suggestion
#define CMP_EPSILON_32 0.000001
#define CMP_EPSILON_64 0.00000000000001
```
author: aaronfranke

body: IMO, if the `double` version of `is_zero_approx` is used in a version of Godot compiled with doubles, and yet you want such a high epsilon to be used *by default* due to some kind of truncation, it means the code calling it is wrong, or it should be using the `float` version all the time, or should use a custom threshold. These unusual cases should be explicit. I don't think hiding the issue solves it.
author: Zylann

body:
All good points, but I am thinking it would be a risky change. As you mentioned, some code calling it could be wrong, or somewhere down the line some piece of code can be truncating to 32-bit floats. It seems like these kinds of problems are worth finding and fixing, but doing it by default seems risky if a bug appears in production. Also, this `double` method is available to builds of Godot where real_t is float.

I think we'll have to ask @akien-mga or @reduz what their thoughts are on this.
author: aaronfranke

body:
Zylann has convinced me that we do need to use the stricter double tolerance in the `is_equal_approx` and `is_zero_approx` methods that uses doubles.

If we decide to keep the methods as they are in this PR, with the double methods using an epsilon more precise than what 32-bit floats are expected to have in error, it would still be a nice idea to have stricter epsilon values available when bug hunting.

```suggestion
#ifdef PRECISE_MATH_CHECKS
#define CMP_EPSILON_32 0.000001
#define CMP_EPSILON_64 0.00000000000001
#else
#define CMP_EPSILON_32 0.00001
#define CMP_EPSILON_64 0.000000001
#endif // PRECISE_MATH_CHECKS
```
author: aaronfranke

body: True, it depends how we want to see `CMP_EPSILON`, because it really isn't THE epsilon from `float` and `double` numbers, it's rather some sort of convenience one that got implicitely used everywhere since every decimal number was a float, but because of that it's getting problematic to decide how to fix problems when we actually have large doubles to deal with.
author: Zylann

body:
Maybe we should only use the 64-bit epsilon if we both pass in doubles and Godot is compiled with doubles?

```suggestion
#ifdef REAL_T_IS_DOUBLE
		return abs(s) < CMP_EPSILON_64;
#else
		return abs(s) < CMP_EPSILON;
#endif // REAL_T_IS_DOUBLE
```
author: aaronfranke

body:
I think the safest option is to only use doubles for the epsilon if both of these are true:
* A double is passed into the methods.
* Godot is compiled with real_t as double.
```suggestion
#ifdef REAL_T_IS_DOUBLE
		return abs(s) < CMP_EPSILON_64;
#else
		return abs(s) < CMP_EPSILON_32;
#endif // REAL_T_IS_DOUBLE
```

body: Negative `screen_rect` should not be possible in a first place, but even if it is, it should be fine to set window position to be it such screen rect. I guess the real issue is caused by the Linux/X11 `DisplayServer`s `set_window_position` and `screen_get_position` working in the different coordinate system for some reason. Also, `0 x 0` point can be outside of any screen as well, I have display config like this, for example:
author: bruvzg

body:
I guess most likely `scene_rect` is not negative, but `pos:0 x 0, size:0x0` (can be the case if `XineramaQueryExtension` call failed) and negative values are caused by subtracting window size from the `0x0`.

Maybe it's better to check if `sceen_rect` size is zero, and simply remove `--position` from the command and rely on window manager to set a reasonable position.
author: bruvzg

body:
>> Just for added clarity I am not using Xinerama. My GPU's and XScreens are segregated to run separate workloads and screens.
> From #58178

And, `XineramaQueryExtension` failing seem to be expected outcome in this case.

Also, the same `screen_get_size(0) - window_size) / 2` is used in the multiple other places (in `Window` for popups, in `DisplayServers` for setting default main window position,  in `ProjectManager` and for editor scale auto-detection), these should check for invalid screen size as well.

body:
Tested locally (without rebasing), it mostly works as expected. This is a feature I'm eager to see, great work so far :slightly_smiling_face: 

There are two significant issues I noticed on a Linux + AZERTY setup:

- Numpad keys can't be used to enter numbers, regardless of Num Lock status. This also applies to the minus symbol to invert the operation (which works in Blender on my end).
- Number row keys can be entered without pressing <kbd>Shift</kbd> (like on QWERTY), while AZERTY normally requires this. This is inconsistent with Blender's behavior on the same setup (tested on Blender 3.5).
  - Blender does not allow pressing <kbd>6</kbd> without <kbd>Shift</kbd> to invert the operation though (this maps to <kbd>-</kbd> on AZERTY). I think we should support that on AZERTY :slightly_smiling_face: 

For this PR to be most usable as a daily driver, I think both issues need to be fixed before it's merged.
author: Calinou

body:
Tested locally, it works better now, but some keys still don't work correctly:

- All numpad keys work, *except* Numpad Minus which doesn't add a `-` symbol before the transformation.
- Only these top row keys work when holding <kbd>Shift</kbd>: `2`, `6` (inverts transformation while it should insert `6` instead), `7`, `9`, `0`. When you don't hold <kbd>Shift</kbd>, all these keys work too, which is not expected on AZERTY. The `.` symbol on the main keyboard (i.e. not on the numpad) doesn't work either to add a decimal.
author: Calinou

body:
AZERTY keys now work as expected :slightly_smiling_face: 

However, I noticed two other issues. I'm not sure if they were by today's changes, I may have started noticing them just now.

- When you press <kbd>-</kbd> to reverse the direction, it applies to future transforms as well. This means that if you use `GX-2` then use `GY2`, the node will be moved *downwards* by units because it's actually translating on the Y axis by `-2`, not `2`. I'd expect it to only affect the current transform operation.
- When you press <kbd>-</kbd> to reverse the direction, it doesn't display a minus symbol in the rotate text in the bottom-left corner. This does not occur with mouse-induced rotation, or with the translate/scale numeric transforms. This is purely a label issue – it doesn't affect how the rotate numeric transform actually behaves.
author: Calinou

body: @aaronfranke Would this be an intended use of the Time singleton?
author: twaritwaikar

body:
Yes. To keep it simple, the Time singleton does not deal with timezone offsets or suffixes, so including them manually is the way to do it (assuming you do in fact need the offset).

However, note that it's convention to not put a space between the timestamp and offset (so it would be `"%s%s"`), and for zero offset it's common to abbreviate this to `Z` (so if `p_offset_minutes` is zero, we can optionally do a version with `"%sZ"`)

Another consideration, we could potentially shorten this using `get_time_string_from_unix_time`, or maybe there should be a dedicated method for converting an offset number to a string - even if the rest of the methods are unaware of timezones, we can add a method to do the same logic you're doing here for the suffix. We just need to think about things like if we want it to print `Z` or `+00:00` and if we want the input value to be in minutes or seconds.
author: aaronfranke

body: I opened #58485 for this.
author: aaronfranke

body:
Thanks for adding the offset to string conversion to `Time`, I will rebase this PR once that change is merged. 

The reason for me not going for a `%sZ%s` is that Git usually shows the `<space>+05:30` format rather than the one with a `Z`, so I will probably just change it to directly use the offset to string conversion function. 

Since the VCS integration is closely (but not directly) mimicing Git, for the time being I am trying to incorporate "Git-likeness" in it if it seems fine, and that is up until the time where we work on other VCS plugins that would require us to further generalise the time format used in the VCS integration in the future, amongst tons of other generalizations that we'd need to make in the UI.
author: twaritwaikar

As with the other PR, the only notable behavior changes are all things that we don't care much about precision of, such as comparisons with `0.99` or `0.0001`.

body:
This code is sus. It's calling `absd` (absolute double) on a `real_t`. It should be calling `abs` instead. Same with the two calls on line 391.

Also, this value would likely make more sense as `CMP_EPSILON` or just `1e-6`, since `1e-7` is so small of an epsilon to the point of not being very useful (`float` precision around 1 is 2^-23 or about `1.192e-7`). It's possible that `1e-7` works for this case, but it's very sus.

The same problems also exist in master.
author: aaronfranke

author: m4gr3d

body:
For numeric types, from what I read it seems that using `type="text" inputmode="..."` is a better option. Using `type="number"` sanitizes `elem.value` to a floating point number (or empty) per the [HTML spec](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/input.html#number-state-(type=number)). We should leave number parsing/validation to Godot users if they want it.

For `type="url"` and `type="email"`, the browser is also supposed to sanitize `value` by stripping leading and trailing whitespace. This isn't as invasive as `type="number"` so I think it's okay to use?

I'll switch to using `type` for the rest.

body:
> If this is something that's needed in the future, sure.

The publicly exposed methods are used by Godot Android plugins, and/or projects that use the engine as a library. So if we're expanding the set of supported keyboard options, we need to provide a mechanism to query the selected keyboard type, hence the addition of the new method.

> this would require adding a redundant int->enum mapping for a privately used member.

Not sure I understand what you mean by `redundant int->enum mapping`.
You can either declare the enum with the same mapping as the native version which will allow to use the int value as index into the java enum values.
Or you can add a private `int` field to the declared enum class, and add logic to perform the mapping.

Both approaches are fairly common in native/java projects and should be straightforward.

Looks good.

Lightmaps will break in some cases, especially in the lower LODs, but the same thing can happen with regular textured meshes and vertex colors so UV2s should not be an exception. There's always the option to disable auto LOD for a given mesh.

On top of these changes, we could look into using the mipmaps in the lightmap texture to store different versions of the lightmap that account for the various mesh LODs. It won't be perfect if there are UV2 overlaps, but we can at least store the average of the overlap, so it doesn't stand out as much.

body:
Describe what embedding subwindows does more in detail:

```suggestion
			If [code]true[/code], the viewport will embed [Window]s. When [Window]s are embedded, [Window]s will [i]not[/i] be actual windows from the window manager's point of view, and they will not be able to leave the main window's viewport. If [code]false[/code], [Window]s will be created separately from the main window, allowing them to be moved outside the main window (and alt-tabbing through [Window]s if desired).
```

Radiance size isn't so much a speed optimization as a memory optimization (which can impact speed in certain scenarios).

It's probably worth doing the same for the `ACCESS_RESOURCES` as well.

By default, `DirAccess` uses process current directory, it's fine for `ACCESS_FILESYSTEM`. But current directory can be changed by calling `OS::set_cwd` or by third party code. If it's changed to be outside PCK location, `ACCESS_RESOURCES` will have the same issue.

Allow more granular panning strength changes:

```suggestion
	ADD_PROPERTY(PropertyInfo(Variant::FLOAT, "panning_strength", PROPERTY_HINT_RANGE, "0,10,0.01"), "set_panning_strength", "get_panning_strength");
```

Also, if values greater than (say) 2.0 are rarely needed, I'd suggest using the following range hint instead:

`"0,2,0.01,or_greater"`

This makes the slider more precise, but still allows users to enter higher values manually.

body:
This project setting doesn't seem to be read when it's changed at run-time, so the effective global panning strength can't be changed at run-time (changes will be ignored). This should be done to make forcing non-positional audio output easier for accessibility. This is not critical for a first iteration of this feature, but in this case, the limitation should be documented in the property description.

See https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/53296#pullrequestreview-877227747 which added a `project_settings_changed` signal in `3.x` for reference (it's also available in `master`).
author: Calinou

body: Same remark as above for this project setting.
author: Calinou

body:
Property range hints should be added with `custom_prop_info[]` (as done above) to specify a valid range for the setting.

I suggest allowing values between 0.0 and 4.0 and with a step of 0.01 (same for 2D and 3D).
author: Calinou

author: ellenhp

body: I think I'd prefer to leave this as-is for now. If we decide that we need to have this update in real-time we can come back and make that change later. It won't break compat so I'm not too worried. Especially considering that we have Stereo Enhancer. Let me know if you'd like me to build in the cache invalidation now though!
author: ellenhp

body:
Is there a reason the 2D and 3D defaults are different? I find the 2D default pretty good, and I'd like the 3D default to be the same in 4.0. The current 3D default feels way too strong to me. Halving the value in the testing project gives me a good result on headphones, but I haven't tested it on speakers.

Over the years, I've seen a lot of complaints about 3D audio panning strength being too strong in Godot, and many of those complaints are likely coming from speaker users.
author: Calinou

body: The 2D and 3D values control fundamentally different values. I'm exposing the tightness parameter in SPCAP, and then just a scalar for panning in 2D. They're not comparable values, so I don't know how to make them the same.

body:
@akien-mga This returns sorted animation names, as desired (so its first element is indeed what we want):
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/9904a9db5a90f2f1896b6b7e1603ca2dd2745bad/scene/resources/sprite_frames.cpp#L105-L112

But in other places in the source code `SpriteFrames::get_animation_list()` is being used instead and the obtained list is being sorted separately:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/9904a9db5a90f2f1896b6b7e1603ca2dd2745bad/scene/resources/sprite_frames.cpp#L99-L103
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/9904a9db5a90f2f1896b6b7e1603ca2dd2745bad/editor/plugins/sprite_frames_editor_plugin.cpp#L985-L987

Both approaches seem to result in the same ordering (both end up comparing with `is_str_less`). But I think we should be consistent and use one over the other. I'd guess sorting a list is probably slower (it probably doesn't matter though) but in the list version there's no StringName -> String conversion (which is also cheap I guess). So is any of these approaches preferred?

body:
I'm not fond of adding exceptions (as they might be forgotten when other things change), but in this case the proper solution would be quite complex, so doing this is be fine. Just the suggested change should be enough.

For reference, the actual problem is that the `int()` constructor has multiple signatures with the same number of parameters but different types. GDScript find the `int(int from)` constructor first, so it thinks the parameter has a narrowing conversion. GDScript should prefer the `int(float from)` constructor, but to do so it would require to know about the multiple types and choose the best match based on those.

It's not something trivial to add, and not really necessary, so for now it's okay to have the exception.

body:
I approve the idea, though this needs a rebase. Not sure if the naming scheme is ideal, though. It's a bit hard to appreciate the difference between `set_margin`, `set_margin_all`, and `set_margin_individual`.

A web developer in me would probably accept `set_margin_all` that accepts 1, 2 or 4 arguments, and depending on the amount it sets all of them/pairs by axis/individuals. But that would likely be very unconventional for Godot API.

So I don't have a better suggestion. We do use these variants quite a lot, though, so addition definitely makes sense. We could avoid exposing `*_individual`, I guess, since user code will unlikely be as verbose as our generated themes.

body:
This is a good idea, and you have done a good job identifying the edge cases. 

We should do two further things:
1. This should use shader versions instead of dynamic branching. The larger performance benefit will come from VGPR savings. Remember VGPRs are allocated for worst case scenario, so you don't save any using dynamic branching. 
2. We should use some of the simplifications from here https://www.unrealengine.com/en-US/blog/physically-based-shading-on-mobile we already use the analytic simplification instead of the LUT, but we could also use the non-metal simplification for the case of non-metals.

body:
This change isn't needed. 

In Godot, ``roughness`` is used for perceptual roughness. In Filament they distinguish between ``roughness`` and ``perceptual_roughness``. In Filament ``roughness = perceptual_roughness * perceptual_roughness`` therefore ``roughness`` in filament is the same as ``alpha_ggx`` in Godot

body:
I investigated briefly this issue. The code is the same on 3.x branch, but the problem doesn't happen there. The reason is that after popup appears, it steals all the input, so the release event is not received. This isn't the case in 4.0. The events are still received after the popup appears, until it gets focused. I think this was added to fix some popup problems (like menus not receiving hover events), and #58726 is just some unwanted side-effect. There could be more of them actually.

Anyways, the fix is good.

The icon won't update when the theme changes this way. The icon has to be set in `NOTIFICATION_ENTER_TREE` and `NOTIFICATION_THEME_CHANGED` in the `_notification` function for it to update on theme change.

It might be easier to make a separate class for the button instead of creating the button entirely in `EditorInspectorActionPlugin`, because that way it can handle theme changes itself.

body:
```suggestion
				[b]Note:[/b] The function will iterate over the [code]values[/code]. For a dictionary, first, it will replace the [code]_[/code] with the key; Then use the result to find the first matched word; At last, use the value to replace the result (see [method replace]); For an one-dimensional array, like the dictionary, but the index number acts as the key, the element of the array acts as the value; For a two-dimensional array, the first element of the inner array acts as the key, the second one acts as the value.
```

author: Calinou

body:
Since there is no C# counterpart, the GDScript-specific codeblock should be removed. Also, singular `[codeblock]` tag should be used instead:

```suggestion
				[codeblock]
				# Like this string, the placeholders may be replaced in steps.
				var str = "The NPC, named {npc_name}, is {npc_age} years old, he comes from $1, and is a member of [organization_name]..."
				# You may already get the npc info like this.
				var npc_info = {"npc_name": "Tom", "npc_age": 24, "organization_id":021, "npc_equipments": {"head": 001, "left_hand": 012}}
				# As the placeholder is the default one, so we can just format `str` like this.
				str = str.format(npc_info) # "The NPC, named Tom, is 24 years old, he comes from $1, and is a member of [organization_name]..."

				# The list of places may look like this.
				var places = ["GodotDesert", "MountGodot", "GodotRiver", "GodotLake"]
				# This placeholder is "$_" now.
				str = str.format(places, "$_") # "The NPC, named Tom, is 24 years old, he comes from MountGodot, and is a member of [organization_name]..."

				# May find the organization info like this.
				var org_info = [["id", 1 ], ["organization_name", "A"], ["position", 1], ["members", []]]
				# The placeholder now is "[_]".
				str = str.format(org_info, "[_]") # "The NPC, named Tom, is 24 years old, he comes from MountGodot, and is a member of A..."
				[/codeblock]
```

Otherwise, no code block will appear at all if the user chose to display only C# code blocks. This can be confusing.
author: Calinou

body:
On second thought, the code sample actually seems hard to follow for me. I find the comments quite confusing as well, especially since the dictionaries/arrays contain unrelated information. Therefore, I think it would be better to showcase a *minimal* example, even if it's less comprenhensive. Something like:

```gdscript
var string = "The NPC {name} lives in {location}.".format({"name": "Tom", "location": "the village"})
print(string)  # The NPC Tom lives in the village.
```

I wouldn't bother showcasing custom placeholders within the class reference, as it's a very situational use case. 99% of the time, standard named placeholders are all you need, including for internationalization :slightly_smiling_face: 

Also, `str` is not a valid variable name (it's a reserved built-in function), so it should be renamed to `string`.

body:
Multiple underscores are allowed. Each will be replaced by the element key. Although I don't think there's any valid use case for it. The following snippet works:

```gdscript
print("{0000} {1111}".format(["Hello", "World"], "{____}"))  # Hello World
print("{ididid}".format({id=42}, "{___}"))  # 42
```
author: timothyqiu

body:
`Array` elements are not required to use the same type. So it does not have to be a strictly two-dimensional array.

```gdscript
# Prints: User 42 is Godot.
print("User {0} is {name}.".format([42, ["name", "Godot"]]))
```
author: timothyqiu

body:
> find the first matched word

It uses `replace()` so the function will replace all occurrences of that result.

You can also omit the indices when using an array:

```gdscript
# Prints: User 42 is Godot.
print("User {} is {}.".format([42, "Godot"], "{}"))
```

body:
Based on your comment/analysis of the issue [here](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/57943#issuecomment-1070806828), I think this is the correct fix. Even with `exact` being false, pressing `D` should not trigger an action with event `Ctrl+D`. The minimum modifiers should be respected. Pressing `Ctrl+Shift+D` however, would still trigger `Ctrl+D` if `exact` is false, which is the desired behaviour I believe.

However, looking back at the doco which was written, it probably is not worded 100% correct. I added this feature (exact matches) but did not write the doco - so that's at least partially my fault.

> If exact_match is false, it ignores the input modifiers for [InputEventKey](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/classes/class_inputeventkey.html#class-inputeventkey) and [InputEventMouseButton](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/classes/class_inputeventmousebutton.html#class-inputeventmousebutton) events, and the direction for [InputEventJoypadMotion](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/classes/class_inputeventjoypadmotion.html#class-inputeventjoypadmotion) events.

It would probably be more correct to say 

> If exact_match is false, it ignores _additional_ input modifiers for InputEventKey... [etc]

author: bruvzg

body:
It seems like a mistake to require both ABS L and R in order to determine whether a device is a gamepad or not. This definition will leave a lot of devices unsupported that people would expect (previously mentioned joycons, for example.)

My 2 cents on the matter here is that we should probably take a page out of SDLs book and be more pragmatic about classifying the feature-set of a given device before filtering the device. We then create a few different masks that we deem to be the "bare minimum gamepad" based on some of the gamepads on the market that have limited feature sets. Additionally, we should consider making a "excludes from gamepad" mask that filters out problematic devices that often have some input overlap with game controllers (drawing tablets, etc.)

So the breakdown of what should be done here on a design level

- Create an enum listing all possible device features that forms a mask. This includes buttons, axis, touch screen, touch pad, stylus, etc. 
- Create a method that returns the feature set of a given device with a breakdown of all possible features. It is worth noting in the function name that this is a guess, not a concrete definition and all future modification to guess work would need to be done in this given method. 
- Define a constant that defines a (few) minimum viable controllers. For example, we currently (in master) would have a set of the following minimum requirements  `HAS_KEYS | HAS_AXIS | HAS_AXIS_LEFT` and `HAS_KEYS | HAS_AXIS | HAS_AXIS_RIGHT`. This creates a easy to parse minimum set of required features for contributors to modify later as more edge cases crop up. 
- Define a constant that defines a feature blacklist. Any device that has these features are automatically removed from being considered valid. For example, filtering out tablets would involve filtering devices with `HAS_STYLUS`. This gives us a concrete list of features that we decide as a group as being "exclusive" from being considered a game controller. 
- Algorithm of `open_joypad` would roughly follow the logic of 
  - Guess feature-set by calling previously mentioned method; Base the logic of guesses off SDL procedure if necessary.
  - Match against any minimum viable controller flag combo, return and close if we don't match any of the minimum requirements.
  - Return and Close device if we match against any gamepad feature on the blacklist. This will help remove laptop touch screens and graphics tablets from listings.

So, in short, I think that we should design from the ground up with regards to improving the input situation and start by trying to get a better understanding of the USB device's entire feature-set before attempting to enforce rules.

The note is not perfectly accurate. The problem is basically that
```GDScript
var time = 0
func _process(delta):
    time += delta
    if time == 2:
        print("end")
```
will never be true due to delta precision (not floating point). I wasn't sure how to word it.

body: I guess an over-simplication could just be, is PoolVector thread-safe or not?  It seems to attempt to be thread-safe as ` PoolVector<T>::operator[]` uses 'read' access, `PoolVector<T>::set` uses a 'write' access, etc.    If that is indeed the case, it would just be so much simpler to put a mutex on the object, and a std::lock_guard at the start of each api call that PoolVector exposes, which is pretty thin tbh.  And most of the interface into actually using a PoolVector is through read & write access, which is partially doing that anyways right now.

author: lawnjelly

body:
I couldn't see any need for the `Access`es to be virtual, as afaik they are are used directly in client code. If there's no need for a vtable etc it makes sense to remove it.

But this is a good point, I have now made the `Access` private to confirm and enforce this. :+1: 

Essentially the virtual is only required if the `Read` or `Write` would be referred to through an `Access` pointer. And this doesn't happen anywhere in the engine.
author: lawnjelly

body: I can't see an immediate reason why it needs to be virtual. Nothing is currently deriving from `PoolVector`.
author: lawnjelly

body: All destructors should be virtual unless you have a REALLY good reason not to.  Making the destructor private doesn't matter.  If someone casts a subclass object (Read or Write here) to an `Access`, then say 'deletes' it as the access reference, it won't call the correct destructor of the instantiated child class.
author: jordo

body: Oh i see what you mean.  Make the entire `Access` class private?

body:
I like the idea of having multiple executables and a single PCK, but not so sure about implementation. Maybe it's better to have some set of pre-defined patterns for the architecture suffix (I would prefer to use something more readable, like "Gama Name (x86_64).exe" instead of multiple extensions).

Or ever do it in opposite direction, something like:
- get the list of PCK in the executable folder first.
- use `exec_filename.begins_with(pck_file.get_file())` to determine which to load.
author: bruvzg

body: A bit pointless for macOS, since the executable is inside the .app bundle and its name is not visible to the user, and a single executable can contain any number of architectures, but I guess it's OK for consistency.
author: bruvzg

body:
The implementation in this PR has the goal of being as simple as possible while allowing these extra use cases. Indeed this will still work fine for macOS. In fact, if the executable is extensionless, this should end up loading on the first try, skipping all of the other logic that would be pointless.

Plus this adds flexibility in the event that someone wanted to not use .app bundles. Even if nobody uses that... it doesn't matter, we don't need to use every possibility of a feature to have a flexible solution be worth it.

author: qarmin

body:
```suggestion
		[EditorUndoRedoManager] is a manager for [UndoRedo] objects associated with edited scenes. Each scene has its own undo history and [EditorUndoRedoManager] ensures that each action performed in the editor gets associated with a proper scene. For actions not related to scenes ([ProjectSettings] edits, external resources, etc.), a separate global history is used.
		The usage is mostly the same as [UndoRedo]. You create and commit actions and the manager automatically decides under-the-hood what scenes it belongs to. The scene is deduced based on the first operation in an action, using the object from the operation. The rules are as follows:
		- If the object is a [Node], use the currently edited scene;
		- If the object is a built-in resource, use the scene from its path;
		- If the object is external resource or anything else, use global history.
		This guessing can sometimes yield false results, so you can provide a custom context object when creating an action.
```

body:
I'm all for this! It's unlikely pre-2011 computers are still around and fast enough to run Godot apps.

If pre-nehalem computers are cut from support anyway, it may make sense to use `-march=nehalem` instead of passing the SIMD flags directly (I opened a related GOP for haswell [here (11150)](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/11150)). This would cut support all the same (unless we want to go the dynamic dispatch or fat binary route), but enables a few additional instruction sets which could benefit performance further.

For completion's sake, here are the additional nehalem flags:

<details>
<summary>diff <(g++ -Q --help=target) <(g++ -Q -march=nehalem --help=target)</summary>

```diff
 ❯ diff <(g++ -Q --help=target) <(g++ -Q -march=nehalem --help=target)
31c31
<   -march=                                     x86-64
---
>   -march=                                     nehalem
36,37c36,37
<   -mavx256-split-unaligned-load       [disabled]
<   -mavx256-split-unaligned-store      [disabled]
---
>   -mavx256-split-unaligned-load       [enabled]
>   -mavx256-split-unaligned-store      [enabled]
74,75c74,75
<   -mcrc32                                     [disabled]
<   -mcx16                                      [disabled]
---
>   -mcrc32                                     [enabled]
>   -mcx16                                      [enabled]
144c144
<   -mno-sse4                                   [enabled]
---
>   -mno-sse4                                   [disabled]
155c155
<   -mpopcnt                                    [disabled]
---
>   -mpopcnt                                    [enabled]
177c177
<   -msahf                                      [disabled]
---
>   -msahf                                      [enabled]
189,191c189,191
<   -msse4                                      [disabled]
<   -msse4.1                                    [disabled]
<   -msse4.2                                    [disabled]
---
>   -msse4                                      [enabled]
>   -msse4.1                                    [enabled]
>   -msse4.2                                    [enabled]
195c195
<   -mssse3                                     [disabled]
---
>   -mssse3                                     [enabled]
213c213
<   -mtune=                                     core2
---

```
</details>
