body:
As seen [here](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X86-64#Microarchitecture_levels), SSE and SSE2 are both part of the `x86-64-v1` baseline.

The [GCC documentation](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc-15.1.0/gcc/x86-Options.html) for `-mfpmath=sse` mentions something along these lines as well:

> For the x86-32 compiler, you must use `-march=cpu-type`, `-msse` or `-msse2` switches to enable SSE extensions and make this option effective. For the x86-64 compiler, these extensions are enabled by default.
author: mihe

body:
Then the whole logic should be:
```python
if env.msvc:
    # Embree needs those; it will automatically use SSE2NEON in ARM.
    env_thirdparty.Append(CPPDEFINES=["__SSE__", "__SSE2__"])
    if env["arch"] == "x86_64":
        env_thirdparty.Append(CPPDEFINES=["__SSE3__", "__SSSE3__", "__SSE4_1__", "__SSE4_2__"])
```

body:
So for Emscripten I found out that `-msse*` flags can be passed too.

Here's a test with:
```
#ifdef __SSE2__
#error yay sse
#endif

int main() { return 0; }
```
```
$ emcc main.c -msse4.2
emcc: error: passing any of -msse, -msse2, -msse3, -mssse3, -msse4.1, -msse4.2, -msse4, -mavx, -mavx2, -mfpu=neon flags also requires passing -msimd128 (or -mrelaxed-simd)!
~/tmp/godot/embree (master %)$ emcc main.c -msse4.2 -msimd128
main.c:2:2: error: yay sse
    2 | #error yay sse
      |  ^
1 error generated.
emcc: error: '/home/akien/Godot/toolchains/emsdk/upstream/bin/clang -target wasm32-unknown-emscripten -fignore-exceptions -mllvm -combiner-global-alias-analysis=false -mllvm -enable-emscripten-sjlj -mllvm -disable-lsr --sysroot=/home/akien/Godot/toolchains/emsdk/upstream/emscripten/cache/sysroot -DEMSCRIPTEN -D__SSE__=1 -D__SSE2__=1 -D__SSE3__=1 -D__SSSE3__=1 -D__SSE4_1__=1 -D__SSE4_2__=1 -Xclang -iwithsysroot/include/fakesdl -Xclang -iwithsysroot/include/compat -msimd128 -c main.c -o /tmp/emscripten_temp_jpbtn1l3/main_0.o' failed (returned 1)
```

As you can see on the error line it does set relevant `__SSE*__` flags when passing those args.

This matches what's described on https://emscripten.org/docs/porting/simd.html#compiling-simd-code-targeting-x86-sse-instruction-sets, that we should pass one of these flags together with `-msimd128`. So we could consider setting `-msse4.2` for wasm32 too.

Discussion in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/106319#discussion_r2087141916 (it would need to be disabled for etcpak).

Again this would be for a follow-up PR, it's good if this one keeps its focus on x86_64 and doesn't impact other targets.

body:
It's similar to how `ngettext` is used: https://www.gnu.org/software/gettext/manual/html_node/Plural-forms.html

But unlike `printf`, `vformat` will complain about excess arguments and return empty string. So something like this won't work:

```c++
vformat(TTRN("One item", "%d items", some_integer), some_integer)
```

body:
Or maybe not, but the sentence syntax is confusing.

Adding a missing comma helps, if I understand the intent correctly:
```suggestion
				Returns the transform from the coordinate system of the canvas, this item is in, to the [Viewport]s coordinate system.
```

I would suggest rephrasing as it's pretty difficult to make sense of IMO. I can help formulate but I'm not sure what the intended meaning actually is.
author: akien-mga

body:
Thanks for the offer. As explanation: Transforms are meant to translate between two coordinate systems. In this case
- from the coordinate system of the canvas (CanvasLayer or default canvas of the Viewport), that this CanvasItem is in
- to the coordinate system of the Viewport, that this CanvasItem is in.

You are right, that a comma would be better for clarity. Will update the PR later and also think about how to word this more clearly.

- Execute `mdfind kMDItemCFBundleIdentifier="org.blenderfoundation.blender"` command and append `/Contents/MacOS/Blender` (as the main detection method).
- If failed, following locations can be checked: `/opt/homebrew/bin/blender`, `/opt/local/bin/blender`, `/usr/local/bin/blender`, `/usr/local/opt/blender` and `/Applications/Blender.app/Contents/MacOS/Blender` .

body:
I don't think it's a good idea to add spaces to words that don't have any underscores in them. If I saw "Comment Focus" in the inspector, my inclination would be to type `comment_focus` into a script because in every other property, a space indicates an underscore. That could be confusing for users, so it would be better to rename these properties to include an underscore where needed instead of "fake" renaming them here.

This goes for "commentfocus", "defaultfocus", "defaultframe", "hseparation", "selectedframe", "vadjust", and "separation" (and "normalmap" as well, but that one wasn't done by this PR)
author: fire-forge

body: This should be renamed as well, for the same reasons as above. It's confusing for it to be "Offset" in the inspector and "ofs" in scripts.
author: fire-forge

body:
I'm not sure. We decided to remap things like `vsync` to `V-Sync` and `po2` to `Power of 2` at the very beginning.

The real property path is visible in tooltip and copyable via context menu or dragging to script directly. IMHO, the capitalization process is more like doing extra English localization from the property-name language. It's not guaranteed to be able to convert back.

And it's not always able to convert back even without the remapping process: e.g. both `http_2` and `http2` are `Http 2` after naive capitalization.
author: timothyqiu

body:
I tested it and it works really well. Just a few things need to be changed/fixed:
* The editor grid drawing should be changed to respect `cell_shape` and `cell_offset_axis` settings like the TileMap editor grid does.
* `cell_offset_axis` only works in hexagon mode, so you can't use offset squares like you can in TileMaps.
* The placement position in the editor is off by about half of a cell. This is more apparent when you go into the Top Orthogonal camera mode when drawing on the GridMap. Disabling `cell_center_x/y/z` makes it off by half of a cell in the opposite direction.
author: fire-forge

body:
```suggestion
		<constant name="CELL_SHAPE_SQUARE" value="0" enum="CellShape">
			Rectangular cell shape.
		</constant>
		<constant name="CELL_SHAPE_HEXAGON" value="1" enum="CellShape">
			Hexagonal cell shape.
		</constant>
		<constant name="CELL_LAYOUT_STACKED" value="0" enum="CellLayout">
			Cell coordinates layout where both axis stay consistent with their respective local horizontal and vertical axis.
		</constant>
		<constant name="CELL_LAYOUT_STACKED_OFFSET" value="1" enum="CellLayout">
			Same as [constant CELL_LAYOUT_STACKED], but the first half-offset is negative instead of positive.
		</constant>
		<constant name="CELL_LAYOUT_STAIRS_RIGHT" value="2" enum="CellLayout">
			Cell coordinates layout where the horizontal axis stay horizontal, and the vertical one goes down-right.
		</constant>
		<constant name="CELL_LAYOUT_STAIRS_DOWN" value="3" enum="CellLayout">
			Cell coordinates layout where the vertical axis stay vertical, and the horizontal one goes down-right.
		</constant>
		<constant name="CELL_LAYOUT_DIAMOND_RIGHT" value="4" enum="CellLayout">
			Cell coordinates layout where the horizontal axis goes up-right, and the vertical one goes down-right.
		</constant>
		<constant name="CELL_LAYOUT_DIAMOND_DOWN" value="5" enum="CellLayout">
			Cell coordinates layout where the horizontal axis goes down-right, and the vertical one goes down-left.
		</constant>
		<constant name="CELL_OFFSET_AXIS_HORIZONTAL" value="0" enum="CellOffsetAxis">
			Horizontal half-offset.
		</constant>
		<constant name="CELL_OFFSET_AXIS_VERTICAL" value="1" enum="CellOffsetAxis">
			Vertical half-offset.
		</constant>
```

This is mostly copied from TileSet.xml but with "tile" changed to "cell". "Horizontal" and "Vertical" still need to be changed because they're somewhat ambiguous in 3D. I didn't make the changes myself because I don't want to get it wrong, but this is a good starting point. I'd need to do more testing to make accurate documentation.

body: The note about 2D batching is only valid if 2D batching works when a texture is supplied. Is this the case? cc @lawnjelly
author: Calinou

body:
Yes batching works when texture is applied, so the old comment is correct (if not great grammatically).

Your revised version of the comment, while being better English, may be less accurate. Anti-aliasing hint is not applied on mobile if I remember right (maybe the GLES_OVER_GL define?), so whether or not batching is used is not necessarily to do with whether the user has set this flag (which is a request), it is to do with whether the engine is trying to apply this flag.

It is probably a good idea at the same time to add this to the docs, that the `antialiased` flag is a hint, and in no way guarantees it will happen, it depends on platform and hardware and driver.

I can confirm that this fixes the issue. However, there are a couple of ways to fix this, I'm not sure if this is the best one (although I'm not completely opposed to merging). It really depends on how we want the API to work. For now, I think it should probably be done in GraphEdit since that class is actually responsible for the whole dragging logic. (GraphNode's set_drag and get_drag_from are not exposed and only used by GraphEdit). So here is what we could do: (No guarantees that the following will work) GraphEdit can update `drag_from` via `set_drag` when several nodes are selected and the user begins the drag.
As an alternative, just inserting
```cpp
drag_from = get_position_offset();
```
in `set_selected` also fixes the issue.

body:
This change doesn't make sense semantically. A plane data structure is composed of a normal vector (for 3D, that's a Vector3), and the distance from the origin.

I think that the entire purpose of `vec3_elems` is to make the code more semantic, since Vector3 is used inside of many other structures, so this change doesn't make much sense. Also, since Vector4 isn't used inside of other structures, it probably doesn't make much sense to define `vec4_elems`, since we don't have `rect2_elems` or similar.

body:
The Android export documentation does need to be changed. I personally feel that developers should not be forced to use the same password to sign the apk. This is wrong. For example, use the newly generated keystore file to sign for updating the same package with different names. I guess the APP signed by the keystore file cannot be updated and replaced, because the signature fingerprints are different, which is not conducive to the migration of Android projects migrated from other IDEs.
Moreover, the command parameter "--key-pass" was not added, which caused a signature error when godot ran to an Android device.
The password of the keystore is used as the alias password in order not to conflict with the author's intention as much as possible, although the author's intention is not understood.
I'm not very good at English, I hope you can edit the docs document.
author: lph993

body:
Use the password of the keystore as the default password when the key alias password is empty.
Sorry, I translated my text from the Internet, and there may be parts that are not easy to understand.

body:
TreeItem should have a second property called `parent_visible_in_tree` which is set when TreeItem is added to Tree. The first TreeItem has it always as true, then its children will have `parent_visible_in_tree` true if the parent has both `visible` and `parent_visible_in_tree` set to true. So it's only checked once and propagated as nodes are added. When item changes visibility, child items get notified and update accordingly.

This is how it works in CanvasItem. It can be done in a separate PR, as it requires some more changes.

body:
I actually disagree that this is dictated by Godot's style guide `i++` is far more common than `++i` in the Godot codebase.
```
$ cgrep -r '++[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z_0-9]*).*{' . --exclude-dir=thirdparty |wc -l
548

$ cgrep -r '[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z_0-9]*++).*{' . --exclude-dir=thirdparty |wc -l
8614
```

body:
Good catch that heap List is not appropriate.

But I actually believe these should be Set<> because it is doing .find() followed by .push_back() which will cause an O(N^2) order complexity.

Set is fine because processing data can happen in arbitrary order

The naming `arr` comes from:
```
Array arr = p_mesh->get_surface_arrays(surf_idx);
```
This is currently the conventional name in Godot to use for Array objects holding the surface vertex and index buffers. Other naming suggestions may be welcome but this would affect a lot of import or mesh code.

(commonly used names in Godot code include `a, arr, array, arrays, mesh_arrays`)

body:
This is great work :trophy: .
With the merge of #66938 we should start moving debugger functionalities out of core and into modules.
I think this is a great candidate for that.
We should move the new files (`editor/debugger/editor_expression_evaluator.cpp`, and `editor/debugger/editor_expression_evaluator.cpp`) into a dedicated module (which I suggest we name "debugger_console" since we might want to add more feature to it).
Similarly, the "evaluate" and "evaluation_return" command should be implemented as DebuggerPlugins with its own capture ("console:evaluate" and "console:evaluate_return").
This will keep things clean, and allow to easily disable the plugin in release builds.

There is an example in `modules/multiplayer/editor/multiplayer_editor_plugin.cpp`, but **I can help with the porting if you want**, just let me know.
One tricky thing is the category name which is hardcoded. We can leave it as "Unknown" initially, but we should probably pass the string directly instead of an int which has to be later interpreted.

One extra note, the PR re-adds 2 files which should be completely removed: `modules/visual_script/visual_script.cpp` and `modules/visual_script/visual_script.h`.

body:
That's a good point, but I don't read them as such, and the specific verb here becomes ambiguous to me at least, but it's not critical :)

Edit: I read them in the *active* voice, not the passive voice, so without the "was" part being implied, e.g. the signal in `AnimationMixer` `animation_finished` for example

Especially as it's fired when the project *starts* running, "was" would imply "the project was started and is now finished", as "was" implies completeness, take for example "I was raised in X", or "I was outside"

So maybe `project_running` or `(project_)run_started`, the latter would match the method name that fires it

Edit 2: Granted there *is* `native_run` in the native runner class, which fires in the same way, so it's either way

author: akien-mga

body:
> Using 33 was a typo here AFAICT, it should be 63 like the multiplier, which is what fits in 6 bits.

Yeah, definitely a typo.

> However comparing the RGB565 conversion with RGBA4444, and some sources online, I have the feel that one of the two is handling the channels backwards. Needs to sit down and write the math, or do some tests, I suspect more fixes are needed.

AFAICT it's correct for RGBA4444, and here it's doing BGR565 instead of RGB565 (BTW the variable should be renamed `rgba` -> `rgb`).

E.g. here you can compare R5G6B5 vs B5G6R5:
- https://registry.khronos.org/vulkan/specs/1.3-extensions/man/html/VkFormat.html#_description
- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/dxgiformat/ne-dxgiformat-dxgi_format#constants

`Image::_get_color_at_ofs` decodes these in the same order as they're set in here though.
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/fe01776f05b1787b28b4a270d53037a3c25f4ca2/core/io/image.cpp#L3116-L3130

So if using `set_pixel`/`get_pixel` etc. it should work fine, but if creating an image from a byte buffer / obtaining such buffer from the image then the results could be unexpected for FORMAT_RGB565 as internally the buffer is assumed to be B5G6R5, not R5G6B5.

Not sure if chaning this is feasible at this point, or if it should be just documented in detail.

---

Note there's also RGBE9995 which seems to also do it backwards (compared to what its name suggests) as bitwise it's actually E5B9G9R9. But in this case there's no commonly used R9G9B9E5 format with which it could be confused (like for B5G6R5 vs R5G6B5 where both are common). So at most it's just a little confusing name.
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/fe01776f05b1787b28b4a270d53037a3c25f4ca2/core/io/image.cpp#L3175-L3177
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/fe01776f05b1787b28b4a270d53037a3c25f4ca2/core/math/color.h#L158

body:
This note assumes that `EditorInspector` is the Inspector dock, so it says the user should not try to create a singleton. (But it's actually not.)

`EditorInspector` node can be created & added, but there is not enough methods exposed to scripting to make it usable. e.g. `edit(object)` is not exposed so you can't really make the newly created `EditorInspector` to inspect anything.

I think `EditorInspector` could eventually be tweaked to make it safe to be used by users/plugins :) For now, it's OK to create and use a `EditorInspector` in C++ and modules.

In the long run, I wonder if we should make the antialiased parameters `true` by default. This is generally what is expected nowadays (see line drawing in SVG or CSS). body:
Two problems here:
1. Using a lower form of PCF for further splits results on visibly worse quality shadows, I don't think we should force that on users
2. Sampling textures in if/else blocks can be a problem on older GPUs as the GPU ends up running both sides of the branch (and thus waiting on the results of both texture lookups). There is a reason this section of code is structured to do the texture lookups after the branching. On high-end GPUs the difference likely won't be noticable, but on low end or older GPUs this can really hurt performance. 

A better approach is to do what you did in 4.0 and adjust the ``directional_shadow_pixel_size`` based on which split you are in. This avoids both issues above and should result in the same benefits as your 4.0 PR.
author: clayjohn

body: Looks much better now. Let's discuss this with other rendering contributors and users. I'm concerned that the visual change is too much to justify making this change to a stable release. I see the benefit of making ths shadow blur stable as it is in 4.0, but, in my opinion, the before scenes look a little nicer than after. This is likely due to how PCF works and may not be avoidable. As it is purely a visuel change (performance should be about the same) I want to make sure we get as much input as we can.

body: This is for the argument list size of auto-generated functions (`Receiver Method` in script). If the number of parameters sent (the value of unbinds spinbox will affect it) is not equal to the parameter list size of the receiving method, an error will be reported.
author: Rindbee

body:
The unbinds spinbox will eventually decide how many parameters to unbind.
Its value is actually the unbind size of the newly constructed `callable` (`CallableCustomUnbind`).
This error occurs if it is greater than `0` (the size of the arguments in `focus_entered` is `0`).
```
E 0:00:03:0853   emit_signalp: Error calling from signal 'focus_entered' to callable: VBoxContainer(test.gd)::_on_button_2_focus_entered : Method expected 0 arguments, but called with 0..
  <C++ Source>   core/object/object.cpp:1132 @ emit_signalp()
```
This is actually an error caused by the unbind value of the callable being too large, and the error message is given a wrong prompt.

The unbind number is not considered when calculating the number in the message, of course negative numbers are unreasonable
author: Rindbee

body:
> The unbinds spinbox could get disabled if max is 0, so users wouldn't wonder why it's non-functional.

The maximum value is `0`, which means there are **no** parameters to unbind, that is, the size of the defined signal parameter list is `0`, disable it is reasonable.

body:
Not sure if this is a good idea, it will only print an error, but won't return a default value.

Maybe it's better to add a return argument to the macro:

```c++
#define VALIDATE_YMDHMS(ret)                                                                                                                                              \
	ERR_FAIL_COND_V_MSG(month == 0, ret, "Invalid month value of: " + itos(month) + ", months are 1-indexed and cannot be 0. See the Time.Month enum for valid values."); \
	ERR_FAIL_COND_V_MSG(month > 12, ret, "Invalid month value of: " + itos(month) + ". See the Time.Month enum for valid values.");                                       \
	ERR_FAIL_COND_V_MSG(hour > 23, ret, "Invalid hour value of: " + itos(hour) + ".");                                                                                    \
	ERR_FAIL_COND_V_MSG(minute > 59, ret, "Invalid minute value of: " + itos(minute) + ".");                                                                              \
	ERR_FAIL_COND_V_MSG(second > 59, ret, "Invalid second value of: " + itos(second) + " (leap seconds are not supported).");                                             \
	/* Do this check after month is tested as valid. */                                                                                                                   \
	ERR_FAIL_COND_V_MSG(day == 0, ret, "Invalid day value of: " + itos(month) + ", days are 1-indexed and cannot be 0.");                                                 \
	uint8_t days_in_this_month = MONTH_DAYS_TABLE[IS_LEAP_YEAR(year)][month - 1];                                                                                         \
	ERR_FAIL_COND_V_MSG(day > days_in_this_month, ret, "Invalid day value of: " + itos(day) + " which is larger than the maximum for this month, " + itos(days_in_this_month) + ".");
```

And then use `VALIDATE_YMDHMS("")` in `get_datetime_string_from_dict`, and `VALIDATE_YMDHMS(0)` in the rest of places.

I think you don't have to set `line_width` to 0.

`current_word_size` is set to 0 when `\n` is encountered:

And `line_width` will be set to `current_word_size` when the `WordCache` for this newline is created:

body:
Use more lightweight formatting and postfix type notation (which is what GDScript uses):

```suggestion
				[code]range(n: int)[/b][/code]: Starts from 0, increases by steps of 1, and stops [i]before[/i] [code]n[/code]. Argument [code]n[/code] is exclusive.
				[code]range(b: int, n: int)[/code]: Starts from [code]b[/code], changes by steps of 1, and stops [i]before[/i] [code]n[/code]. Arguments [code]b[/code] and [code]n[/code] are [b]inclusive[/b] and [b]exclusive[/b], respectively.
				[code]range(b: int, n: int, s: int)[/code]: Starts from [code]b[/code], changes by steps of [code]s[/code], and stops [i]before[/i] [code]n[/code]. Arguments [code]b[/code] and [code]n[/code] are [b]inclusive[/b] and [b]exclusive[/b], respectively. The argument [code]s[/code] [b]can[/b] be negative, but not [code]0[/code]. If [code]s[/code] is [code]0[/code], an error message is printed.
```

Having a float step that's not a power of 10 is indeed a weird use case, so I think it makes sense to only allow powers of 10 and expose the exponent here. We could likely rename this to something else, maybe `default_float_decimals`? But if that was the name, we'd have to negate it for it to make sense.

author: Calinou

body:
Well for `SceneShaderGLES2::FADE_TO` I couldn't help noticing we have already sent this value 5 lines above (as `split_offsets`), and each uniform is additional extra API (especially in GLES2), and can compete for limited space in GPU for highly cached stuff.

The only question is which of these 4 is the furthest split, but I'm presuming this must have been previously calculated in the shader (for it to have worked previously).

There are 2 questions that appear:
* Is it more expensive to use the existing `if` clauses than to send as an independent additional uniform (this may be the case, dynamic branches aren't good but maybe we have paid the price already)
* The existing `light_split_offset` is stored as `highp`. This might not be necessary for the calculation so could maybe be downcast.

I guess these can only be answered by performance testing especially on mobile. And I guess could be left to a follow up. I'm not sure whether this was considered in 4.x, but maybe there were additional factors at play.

body:
Just a syntactic note (you don't have to address it in this PR). I've typically seen shader hints written like this:

```glsl
uniform sampler2D color_ramp : repeat_disable;
```

As opposed to this:

```glsl
uniform sampler2D color_ramp: repeat_disable;
```

I don't have a strong opinion on which one is better.

The [Godot shader language style guide](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/tutorials/shaders/shaders_style_guide.html) doesn't make a recommendation on this yet, but we should figure one out.
author: Calinou
fire-forge

body:
```suggestion
				Returns a list of contact point pairs where this shape touches another.
				If there are no collisions, the returned list is empty. Otherwise, the returned list contains contact points arranged in pairs, with entries alternating between points on the boundary of this shape and points on the boundary of [code]with_shape[/code].
				A collision pair A, B can be used to calculate the collision normal with [code](B - A).normalized()[/code], and the collision depth with [code](B - A).length()[/code]. This information is typically used to separate shapes, particularly in collision solvers.
```
author: Calinou

body:
```suggestion
				Returns a list of contact point pairs where this shape would touch another, if a given movement was applied.
				If there would be no collisions, the returned list is empty. Otherwise, the returned list contains contact points arranged in pairs, with entries alternating between points on the boundary of this shape and points on the boundary of [code]with_shape[/code].
				A collision pair A, B can be used to calculate the collision normal with [code](B - A).normalized()[/code], and the collision depth with [code](B - A).length()[/code]. This information is typically used to separate shapes, particularly in collision solvers.
```

author: willnationsdev

body:
Rather than checking for specific languages' extensions, it's better to use `ResourceLoader::get_resource_type(p_path)` to extract a typename and then use an inheritance check to see if it inherits from the `Script` class. If so, you know it's a script, regardless of what language it is.

You can *probably* assume that a user-defined type WON'T be an implementation of a Script, so you could skip it, but ordinarily, you would first check `ScriptServer::is_global_class()` and then based on that either use `EditorNode::get_editor_data().script_class_is_parent()` if true or `ClassDB::is_parent_class()` if false. Note also that you can only use the former if you are in an editor context. If it's something that would be running in a live application outside the editor, then you have to fully load the script and then do `script->get_language()->get_global_class_name(script->get_path())`. It's a bit ridiculous, just because the ScriptServer doesn't do any work to maintain a reverse lookup of path-to-typename.
author: willnationsdev

body: This assumes that all user-defined resources will be things that extend `Resource`. If I chose to implement a script extending, say, `Texture`, then I would be unable to make use of this. Nor if I chose to define a second type extending my first one. So,  rather than checking for an exact match, you have to perform another inheritance check, akin to the process I described in my earlier comment w/ ScriptServer, EditorData, and ClassDB.
author: willnationsdev

body: I believe Godot's conventions generally prefers curly braces, even when they aren't necessary (iirc).
author: willnationsdev

body: From what I can tell, assigning this here is unnecessary(?). You only actually appear to use this value later on when attempting to get the filesystem path, but the way you use it - as `fi.path.get_base_dir()` - should be the same thing as `p_dir->get_path()` which is already assigned to the `lpath` variable declared earlier. And if you do end up needing the actual path, the `file_metadata` appears to already be doing that (and is set as the 0th slot metadata for the `file_item` `TreeItem`). Unless I'm misreading the code.
author: willnationsdev

body: @Atlinx Oh, also, when referring to the String values of type names, it's best to use `<type>::get_class_static()` rather than a hardcoded string. So, in this instance, you'd use `Resource::get_class_static()`.
author: willnationsdev

author: Atlinx

body: @Atlinx Also, I just realized examining this, that this is not really a good solution (or at least, it doesn't seem very reliable). Any given file can have 0 or more dependencies, any number of which might happen to be a script. But you don't know *which* of those dependencies is the `script` property of the file in question. So, imo, you really *do* need to have some sort of resource script type property being tracked in the `FileInfo`/`FileCache` in `EditroFileSystem`. And rather than forcing the use of `ResourceFormatLoaderText`, you would need to define a virtual function in `ResourceFormatLoader` that gets the scripted type of the resource, if it has one, which you would then override in the various derived implementations. Then the logic to get the file's resource script type would involve iterating over all of the loaders present in the `ResourceLoader`.
author: willnationsdev

body: Does this mean we would have to break compatibility with 3.x? Adding another field to `FileInfo`/`FileCache` means incrementing the file cache file version, and I'm not sure if this counts as compatibility breakage. Either way, I'll try to rewrite this PR using your suggestions.
author: Atlinx

body: I'm *guessing* it would involve breaking compatibility. Even if you assume that it's possible for 3.x and 4.x to have their own branching continuations of file cache versions, the fact that they share the same sequential numeric versioning scheme means that as soon as 3.x attempts to increment beyond where it already is, it will be stepping into the realm of 4.x's prior iterations (probably?). So yeah, I doubt this is something that can be properly done in 3.x, simply because the EditorFileSystem doesn't have any mechanism by which it can efficiently keep track of the type of the script that may or may not be assigned to a given resource file.
author: willnationsdev

body:
The ResourceFormatLoader thing might also become a headache *IF* you end up also modifying `get_resource_type` to return a possible non-C++ class as there are many calls to this method throughout the codebase, all of which likely expect it to return an in-engine C++ type rather than a potential scripted type. If you don't change the logic of that method though, and instead define a new function to access the extra information, thereby demanding that any scenario that need it specifically call that separate function, then there's likely to be less conflict with other uses.

I apparently have a habit of coming up with rather large API-changing solutions, but the team generally frowns on doing that kind of thing unless it's absolutely necessary. So probably try to avoid that as much as you can.

body:
1. (unless you were just adding temporary notes for yourself) Generally prefer not including comments unless it specifically is providing additional contextual details for how it interacts with other code elsewhere or perhaps explains why it chose a specific algorithm over another one. The code should be written in such a way that it is largely self-documenting. Overuse of comments leads to not-maintained "documentation" embedded in the code that quickly becomes stale as code is modified over time in large projects.
2. Because `get_path()` and `find_file_index` are member functions (i.e. methods) of the `EditorFileSystemDirectory` class, and we are inside of that class currently, you do not need to preface them with the class name scope; it should automatically call the local implementation. You only do this for `EditorFileSystem::get_singleton()` because that specifically fetches a static instance of the `EditorFileSystem` class and thus can only be accessed via a static getter function to begin with.

body:
@KoBeWi

> As I said, this is not important in most cases. When you create a custom resource the script will always be first

Did you perhaps mean that the script will always be *last*? Because I have a use case in my sample project for #48201 where an exported resource property itself uses a global script class that has another global script class property as its exported property, and in *that* use case, there are 2 `[ext_resource type="Script"...]` tags with the bottom one being the `script` property's `Script` (i.e. the "attached" script). Which then results in @Atlinx's code triggering a bug in my code.

To be specific, my demo has a `Tile2D` node that has a `terrain` property of type `Terrain`. It is possible to load a `WildTerrain` resource onto it (or an `UrbanTerrain`), and the `WildTerrain` has an exported array of `NaturalResource` resources. And in this use case, if I save e.g. a `wild_terrain_1.tres` file, that file needs both `NaturalResource` and `WildTerrain`, but it grabs the wrong one b/c `WildTerrain` is at the bottom and it stops after it finds the first one.

The result is that the `QuickLoad` popup is unable to find the saved resource file because it does not perceive the file to have the `WildTerrain` type.

Either way, this PR needs to be amended to fix that.

body:
(Edit: lol, I started writing this before I saw that you had already responded and seem perfectly at ease with the feedback, but anyway, just in case, I hope you find this explanation helpful, haha)

I wanna thank you again for all the work you put into this PR. Even if it isn't accepted in its current state, it definitely gives a better starting point from which to create a more effective solution.

I've been in past situations where I did a bunch of work for a PR and received a similar response of my changes being too "invasive" or "hacky". At the time, I was quite disheartened and frustrated. This may or may not be the case for you, but if it is, I'm re-reviewing these changes through this lens to consider in what ways others may find fault with them. I hope my feedback can provide insight into what could be improved here and enrich the quality of your future work should you choose to continue making contributions.

- For things labeled as "comment bloat":

    Generally, Godot's source code only contains comments when it is meant to explain *why* the chosen code was selected vs. another approach since it usually is a contextual matter specific to Godot's codebase/behavior. That is, one needs to take into account other code in order to know why the current code is doing what it does.
    
    However, if the current code is self-explanatory or can be discerned simply by reading the code within the class/function and putting the pieces together yourself, then explanatory comments are avoided since they are a maintenance hazard; they can easily become outdated by future contributors' work, and they unnecessarily space out the code making it harder to read as much content within a single view.

- For things labeled as "uncertain/questionable core bloat*" or "core bloat?*":

    This is a pull request that relates to improving the behavior of things at the editor level. As such, the effect it should have on the engine's core should be kept to a minimum. However, in the case of this specific problem, it ultimately has to be resolved by extracting serialized data from resources which, in turn, must involve making changes to the way in which resources are serialized (a piece of core logic). So you can't really *avoid* making *some* core changes to implement a solution (I **think**). If there IS a way to do that, then we need to investigate that possibility and re-work the solution to support that. If not though, I don't personally see any issue with the way you've added the extra behavior to the `ResourceFormatLoader`(s). Rather, the more pertinent issue is probably...(next point)
    
- For things labeled as "core bloat**":

    Variant is an extremely core class since it basically touches every piece of the engine, so any changes made to it are done so with great care. Nonetheless, there are a variety of new methods you've added to its parser which I'm not entirely sure should be necessary just for the sake of implementing this feature.

    Furthermore, the way in which the type information is extracted using the tags is inherently "hacky" since it revolves around figuring out where the list of property tags are, iterating through all of the properties, and identifying which one corresponds to the object's "script" property. This in turn makes the logic entirely dependent on the structural makeup of the tag tree, as well as whether the object in question has a "script" property. Should a later alteration in the engine choose to modify how script information is associated with an object, the VariantParser, of all things, would now suddenly be broken (which would not be directly related to Object-handling code and thus not obvious).
    
    In addition, the algorithm introduces a linear-time operation over the number of properties the object serializes. This contrasts with how, in concept at least, the operation is not at all requesting aggregate data related to the object's properties. It would make more sense for it to be constant-time operation.

None of this is to suggest that you didn't have reasons for doing things the way you did. I honestly probably would've first thought to do something similar to what you did (which is probably why I didn't question it the first time I reviewed it). That is, don't make any major changes to the way data is tracked and just work with what is already there to accomplish what minimum changes are necessary to make the logic further down the pipeline work properly.

In theory, you are attempting to avoid making significant changes to the core. In practice though, given the lengths your implementation has to go through to get things to work as needed, a worrying number of changes are introduced.

In this PR, you introduce changes to the core variant logic *merely* to support this one higher-level feature. If left alone and accepted, such single-purpose solutions culminate in a codebase that has many small pieces in the core that are not actually used by or relevant to the core at all, thus leading to code bloat. It would be preferred if we could make the minimum number of changes possible to the core to expose whatever information is necessary for higher-level pieces of the codebase to implement the required logic. This shifts the bulk of the changes to later layers of the architecture and results in a simpler and more direct approach to achieving the same goal.

I appreciate you going through all the effort of writing and maintaining this branch. It will certainly be a big help in guiding the direction of the next attempt at fixing this issue. Great work!

body:
>when these two angles are 180 degrees apart

Note that these angles are in radians so they can't really be exactly 180 degrees apart (180 degrees = PI radians and PI is irrational). Meaning in such cases the actual difference is always a little smaller/bigger than 180 degrees (by some epsilon).

>In this particular implementation it favors lerping through the negative side.

No idea what do you mean by "_through the negative side_", it's not clear. I'd say it can lerp either clockwise or counter-clockwise. And I think whether it goes clockwise/counter-clockwise depends of the error I've mentioned earlier (whether the actual angle difference is bigger/smaller than 180 degrees).
author: kleonc

body:
> And I think whether it goes clockwise/counter-clockwise depends of the error I've mentioned earlier (whether the actual angle difference is bigger/smaller than 180 degrees).

In practice it's when this is called with a difference of `PI` that the UB happens. But indeed due to floating point precision the result can basically be one or the other, depending on whether `fmod(nearly TAU, TAU)` ends up as "nearly `TAU`" or "nearly 0". So it's not so much an implementation details as unpredictable.
author: akien-mga

body:
> So it's not so much an implementation details as unpredictable.

I think it's predictable if the user dig into how IEEE 754 float point works, but not worth the effort :stuck_out_tongue_closed_eyes:
author: timothyqiu

body:
>when these two angles are approximately [constant @GDScript.PI] apart

To be precise: if angles are `PI + k * TAU` apart for any integer `k`. For example `0` and `99 * PI` angles are also kinda `PI` apart.

```
i=0  COUNTER-CLOCKWISE    lerp_angle(0, PI + 0 * TAU, 0.5) = -1.570796
i=1  COUNTER-CLOCKWISE    lerp_angle(0, PI + 1 * TAU, 0.5) = -1.570796
i=2  COUNTER-CLOCKWISE    lerp_angle(0, PI + 2 * TAU, 0.5) = -1.570796
i=3  COUNTER-CLOCKWISE    lerp_angle(0, PI + 3 * TAU, 0.5) = -1.570796
i=4  COUNTER-CLOCKWISE    lerp_angle(0, PI + 4 * TAU, 0.5) = -1.570796
i=5  CLOCKWISE            lerp_angle(0, PI + 5 * TAU, 0.5) =  1.570796
i=6  COUNTER-CLOCKWISE    lerp_angle(0, PI + 6 * TAU, 0.5) = -1.570796
i=7  CLOCKWISE            lerp_angle(0, PI + 7 * TAU, 0.5) =  1.570796
i=8  COUNTER-CLOCKWISE    lerp_angle(0, PI + 8 * TAU, 0.5) = -1.570796
i=9  CLOCKWISE            lerp_angle(0, PI + 9 * TAU, 0.5) =  1.570796
```

body:
Ah funky, with this change removed `float luminance = dot(frag_color.rgb, vec3(0.299, 0.587, 0.114));` no longer gives the right result, so I've undone that change too.

This was all to get the color brightness right but as explained further down below, I think that is caused by a different issue.

I do wonder if the dot approach for calculating luminance, instead of simply taking the max or r,g or b, is the better approach. We'll have to talk with @JFonS about that one.

For now I've reverted the change, we can always add a specific PR in to improve this.
author: BastiaanOlij

body:
Okay, removing `color /= 1.0 - dot(color.rgb, vec3(0.299, 0.587, 0.114));` and changing the luminance calculation back to what it was results in the mobile renderer blur to be much brighter then the clustered one. A reverse of the previous situation.

However putting it back in the size of the blur become nearly identical with the same intensity values. The problem however is that the mobile renderer is again starting to max out on the glow buffers because you really need to crank up the strength of the glow to get a nice blur and we're hitting max values in the glow buffers themselves now.

Also both with the clustered and mobile renderer, it seems like blue is nearly filtered out, not surprising seeing the values we're doing the dot product with.

I don't know enough about the proper color calculations needed here but Clayjohns remark that the colors aren't tone mapped is spot on, tonemapping doesn't happen until after the glow buffers have been created and applied in the tonemap shader. I'm feeling that what the clustered renderer is doing is actually wrong...

I'm keeping the tonemapping thing in both shaders for now so they are atleast similar in result, however I think this needs more debate (also posted this in `#rendering`)
author: BastiaanOlij

body:
As discussed last week, lets keep the old luminance calculation. I changed it to this one fairly arbitrarily, but the old calculation appears to better reflect the perceived brightness when colors become saturated.

body:
I think "finalize" as proposed in your poll might be a better term. "uninitialize" might be a bit more common than "deinitialize" but it's also used for _uninitialized variable_, which means something which _has not been initialized yet_. While here things have been initialized, used, and now need to be cleaned up (which is usually different from just de-initializing variables).

Actually as I write this, "deinitialize" sounds a bit better to avoid the confusion with _not yet initialized_ "uninitialized". That is if we want to keep "initialize" as part of the name since the enums are about `INITIALIZATION_LEVEL`. Otherwise I think another term like "finalize" can be clearer and less prone to typos (it's always super difficult to spell uninitialize IMO with all those ni's :P).

Alternatively, I like "cleanup" too for this kind of task.

body:
Sorry this is a bit of a mess - it turns out the checks were unnecessary (and didn't do anything) anyway, since the text has already been passed through `rstize_text`. When being edited by `indent_bullets`, the codeblocks will have been converted to rst:

```
::

    codeblock text here
    - leading bullet point characters will be fine
```

where the text is [always indented with spaces, not tabs](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/community/contributing/class_reference_writing_guidelines.html#):

> Use [codeblock] for pre-formatted code blocks. Inside [codeblock], always use four spaces for indentation. The parser will delete tabs. 

and the text on any non-empty line is [preceded by at least four spaces](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/61517b6d3994f419580a89f5ba3270681c442dd6/doc/tools/make_rst.py#L892). `indent_bullets` will see that the first character after any leading tabs is a space, not a bullet point character, and so skip over the line.

I've taken the codeblock checks back out again.
author: snailrhymer

Ring segments' maximum can be left at its current value (64) still.

body:
Since there are no project settings for this, there doesn't seem to be a way to change the VRS mode on the root Viewport without using a script or a custom viewport. This should probably be added by allowing the user to specify the mode for the root Viewport, and a texture path. If changing the mode to XR doesn't make sense for the root Viewport, then the option should be hidden and only Disabled and Texture should be displayed.

The documentation should also mention what format is expected for `vrs_texture`:

- Which color format should the image have? I assume it's `Image.FORMAT_RGB8`, but this should be mentioned explicitly, especially if other modes don't work.
- Which colors should be used to represent individual shading rates?
- What resolution should the texture be? Can it have a different resolution or aspect ratio than the viewport?
- What happens if a texture with invalid colors is used? Is the nearest color used instead, or is an error printed? (If not, there should probably be some kind of validation in debug builds. In any case, the "invalid" behavior should be documented.)

___

Also, I think a global VRS override should be supported for performance testing purposes. While this is generally not meant for use in production, it can be used to see how much performance you could gain from enabling VRS in theory.

This could be a list of premade textures with all pixels set to 1×2, 2×1, 2×2 and so on. This would also be good to expose as a VRS mode in the project settings. This can be done in a future PR though – I'll open a proposal in that case, so we remember to do it in the future.
author: Calinou

body:
I think the biggest issue especially with VRS on XR is that you generally don't want to turn it on until it is available. With non-XR use cases the texture may not be available from the start especially if the texture is not a fixed texture but one generated (at the moment only possible for code, but we should investigate some day creating viewports based on different texture formats including R8_NORM

It's also very likely that you wouldn't turn VRS on in a menu screen, but do turn it on in the main viewport when your game actually starts (especially in non-XR use cases), that likely will only be a feasible option to do in code.

That all said, out of principle having these settings available as project settings, even if only to make it clear this functionality is supported on the main viewport, and for making it easier to run test projects, yeah it may not be a bad idea to just add them and accept they have limited uses.
author: BastiaanOlij

But I'm not sure about reading everything to the buffer and converting it at once. This will prevent receiving long output in portions (e.g., from the other thread).

body:
> In particular, I had to specify a default capture mode to get it to compile for some parts that were using GLOBAL_GET.

You can pass `SETTING_NAME` in as a parameter to avoid capturing anything.

```suggestion
#define GLOBAL_GET_FAST(VAR_TYPE, SETTING_NAME) ([](const StringName &p_name) -> const VAR_TYPE & {\
	static VAR_TYPE local_var;                                                   \
		static uint32_t local_version = 0;                                      \
		uint32_t new_version = ProjectSettings::get_singleton()->get_version(); \
		if (local_version != new_version) {                                     \
			local_version = new_version;                                        \
			local_var = ProjectSettings::get_singleton()->get(p_name);     \
		}					                                                   \
		return local_var; })(SETTING_NAME)
```

Also maybe this macro should be `GLOBAL_GET_CACHED` since `GLOBAL_GET_UNCACHED` is used?
author: timothyqiu

body:
Ah excellent I'll try that with the parameter. :+1: 

On the naming, I'm open to suggestions. When I talked with @akien-mga  earlier we were going with the idea of replacing the existing `GLOBAL_GET` wholesale for most cases, so no changes to existing code were needed. The uncached version I added for "backward" support, but it isn't used anywhere so could potentially be removed.

So that mainly leaves what to call the `FAST` version. I did think about `GLOBAL_GET_CACHED` but, both are cached now, so the only difference is that this version stores a type instead of a variant. It could be called e.g. `GLOBAL_GET_TYPED`, but then that isn't very clear from the use areas _why_ it might be used in preference to `GLOBAL_GET`. But I'm welcoming any ideas here.

Ah, not sure I missed this, but, `gutters.size()` should be identical to `text[p_line].gutters.size()`. However, `[add|remove]_gutter` loops over every line in both `TextEdit` and `TextEdit::Text` so we end up removing the same amount of gutters as there are lines.

Probably best to remove the loops in `TextEdit` not `TextEdit::Text`. Might also be worth adjusting the test cases to catch this in the future.

Sure! I find option 3 to be easiest, because pressing "Commit suggestion" makes a new commit which you then have to squash with the other commit. If you have to do that for several suggestions, then it makes a lot of commits to squash. 

"Resolve conversation" is for when the change is made, its decided that the change doesn't need to be made, or the change doesn't apply anymore. So here you can press it after you commit the suggestion.

This looks OK to me on the surface, but going through all scenes if you want to delete or rename a group seems very unfeasible to me. In a large project this could take an hour and become unusable.

I would try to think about other ways to do this, such as:
* Cache in EditorFileSystem the global groups used (that will probably be a good amount of work to do), so only the relevant scenes are changed.
* Find another way to do it, such as leaving the group remain there but consider it invalid on load if it does not exist (just ignore it). This seems probably like the easiest way.

body: Actually, I looked over this one, and no, the reason we keep it in an array is to revert naming conflicts. In the incident, that the node gets reparented to another node when its owner has its editable status removed, it may conflict with a matching name. By storing the names in an array, it means it can correctly restore its original name upon undoing.
author: SaracenOne

body:
While it seems overkill, I can explain why we have the names and transforms array. For explanation of names, see the explanation to the above response about undoing naming conflicts. As for the transforms, while its uncommon given a revert chain should put things back to its relative position before the reparent, storing the original transforms will restore it in situations where the local position might change. In example I was testing, I had a scene with an animated skeleton and bone attachments. If I instance this scene in another scene, make it editable, then attach a mesh to that bone attachment, then remove the editable status, the mesh will become detached. This is fine, but if the skeleton is animating, upon undoing the action to remove the editable children status, we don't know how to restore it local position by just using reparenting, hence the use of the transform array.

Admittedly, yeah, it this wasn't doing anything because the code to restore the local transform got lost during the migration to the reparent function, but I've restored it now.
author: SaracenOne

body:
So I'm a little confused what you mean. The if statement is basically meant to check if the nodes in the array are parented to nodes owned by the instance we just removed editable children status from.

Admitedly though, after looking over it, I think this was the wrong place to put it. Instead, I've put it up in _toggle_editable_children when constructing the initial arrays, because otherwise, there's discontinuity between the two methods and this check doesn't reflect the description of what this specific method is meant to do.

body:
I think you could remove the need for binding this method in `_bind_methods` (which makes it accessible from scripting, even if hidden by default due to the leading `_`) by using Callables instead of StringNames:

```suggestion
	callable_mp(this, &Popup::_popup_conditional_hide).call_deferred({}, 0);
```

CC @KoBeWi to confirm, currently we seem to only use `Callable::call_deferred` on a `callable_mp` once:
```
scene/3d/collision_object_3d.cpp
322:                    callable_mp(this, &CollisionObject3D::_update_debug_shapes).call_deferred({}, 0);
```

I noticed the Windows crash handler is disabled in release builds (also on macOS), but the Linux crash handler is available in release builds. This is needed to make https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/61906 effective in exported projects.

I suggest we always make the crash handler available in release builds, as release builds can be compiled with debug symbols enabled (or you can use a tool to reverse them if you have access to the debug symbols).

body:
I saw you were asking on RC about adding comments. So for me comments like this one are just a noise. Method is trivial and I think it's obvious what it does just after a brief look at the code. I'd say don't tell what the code does unless: it's not easy to understand, or you're referring to some bigger chunk of code in a summary-like manner, or you're sectioning code with a title-like comments, etc. Simple comments to help with quick glancing at the code and more detailed ones when explaining how something works / why it's done like that (but again: no reason to explain how 2+2 works as well as no reason to tell it's an addition). Of course there's no golden rule and that's just my opinion. :wink:

Also there's `String::trim_prefix` you can use:

```suggestion
static String strip_resource_schema(const String &from) {
	return from.trim_prefix("res://");
```

I don't know if it's desired to have such one-liner specialization of `trim_prefix`. No idea whether there's some preference / guidelines about it.

author: derammo

body:
> I could not leave it, because it causes the popup to get pushed off screen. However, I also could not fix it, because it isn't clear what it is trying to do. There is a comment that it is trying to put the popup within the bounds of the graph.
>
> I can't figure out why this would have been a requirement? Surely if the main window is currently small on the screen, you don't want the popup to try to fit inside it? It is a floating window.

You have to remember that Godot before 4.0 did not support multiple windows. So all dialogs were embedded in the main window (which can be reproduced in 4.0 by enabling single window mode).

So this code probably tries to make sure that the dialog in 3.x and in single window mode in 4.0 fits within the bounds of the graph editor. Since windowing changed in 4.0, this may no longer be needed, or may indeed be done wrong. So it can either be removed completely if the issue it tries to solve is no longer an issue, or fixed.

I'd suggest removing, after checking how things work in Single Window Mode.
author: akien-mga

body: CC @swarnimarun who implemented that code in case you remember why it's needed and why it's done this way. But I strongly suspect the DisplayServer and multi window support changes in 4.0 pretty much invalidate what it was trying to do, so the simplest is probably to remove it if it causes issues, and make a new fix if there are still problems (e.g. in Single Window Mode).
author: akien-mga

body: Great info, thanks.  Will do.
author: derammo

body:
@akien-mga was right.  The code I disabled doesn't do anything bad in ```--single-window```.    It also doesn't do much of anything important.  In all my tests the embedded window was already aligned within the graph area.  In multi window, it does bad things because:

Window::set_position(...) in single window mode takes coordinates relative to the main window, presumably like 3.x.

Window::set_position(...) in multi window takes absolute screen coordinates, so things can end up on an entirely different monitor for example.  I am going to start a separate discussion on that, but it is the reason why I initially said the calculation is wrong (for 4.x).

I tested it on both single and multi, and it is better to just remove the code and let the popup code position things, because it is single/multi aware.

body:
I'd mention an example, since HTML5 with threads disabled is likely the most common configuration that doesn't support threads:

```suggestion
				Bakes the [NavigationMesh]. If [code]on_thread[/code] is set to [code]true[/code] (default), the baking is done on a separate thread. Baking on a separate thread is automatically disabled on operating systems that cannot use threads (such as HTML5 with threads disabled). Baking on separate thread is useful because navigation baking is not a cheap operation. When it is completed, it automatically sets the new [NavigationMesh]. Please note that baking on separate thread may be very slow if geometry is parsed from meshes as async access to each mesh involves heavy synchronization.
```
author: Calinou

body: It would be better to put that new sentence at the end so that it would be something like `Please note that (...). Also, please note that baking on a separate thread is automatically disabled (...).`
author: Scony

body:
With the above approach, it is possible the user will ask navigation mesh to bake on the thread but Godot will silently do it off thread. IMHO that's wrong. As a user, in the situation when I `bake(true)` and the platform does not support threading, I'd expect a warning (in case of fallback to off-thread baking) or error (in case we block such scenario entirely).

I guess lots of users used to not care about warnings, so a warning about auto fallback may be overlooked - yet it may have a severe impact on performance. Therefore I'd say we should issue an error asking one to not use `on_thread=true` in such case.
author: Scony

body: I guess a user warn_print() in the editor cannot hurt while a red error might be too much. Should still turn threaded baking off automatically in my opinion cause on_thread is true by default. A crash or not baked navmesh is worse than a framerate stutter. If framerate has problems on certain export platforms users usually begin to look at warnings and logs to notice this problem.

body: Ok, let's add warning - if someone will complain we will make it an error then.

The parse behaviour can be controlled with the [member NavigationMesh.geometry/parsed_geometry_type] and [member NavigationMesh.geometry/source_geometry_mode] properties on the [NavigationMesh] resource.

author: smix8

body:
For "parseable" vs "parsable", both uses seem valid but the latter seems recommended: https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/parsable

```suggestion
		Navigation mesh baking happens in multiple steps and the result depends on 3D source geometry and properties of the [NavigationMesh] resource. In the first step, starting from a root node and depending on [NavigationMesh] properties all valid 3D source geometry nodes are collected from the [SceneTree]. Second, all collected nodes are parsed for their relevant 3D geometry data and a combined 3D mesh is build. Due to the many different types of parsable objects, from normal [MeshInstance3D]s to [CSGShape3D]s or various [CollisionObject3D]s, some operations to collect geometry data can trigger [RenderingServer] and [PhysicsServer3D] synchronizations. Server synchronization can have a negative effect on baking time or framerate as it often involves [Mutex] locking for thread security. Many parsable objects and the continuous synchronization with other threaded Servers can increase the baking time significantly. On the other hand only a few but very large and complex objects will take some time to prepare for the Servers which can noticeably stall the next frame render. As a general rule the total amount of parsable objects and their individual size and complexity should be balanced to avoid framerate issues or very long baking times. The combined mesh is then passed to the Recast Navigation Object to test the source geometry for walkable terrain suitable to [NavigationMesh] agent properties by creating a voxel world around the meshes bounding area.
```

body:
This isn't quite correct. The color space in which they are processed has to do with what color space they are stored in (which is controlled by the user) 

``vertex_color_is_srgb`` flags the material to convert the vertex colors from srgb space into linear space. 

``vertex_color_is_srgb`` should be set whenever vertex colours are specified in srgb, otherwise it should be false.

The description under the ``FLAG_SRGB_VERTEX_COLOR`` constant is much more accurate

body:
Sorry for not looking at this sooner. It does look like this is improving issues that the SkeletonModificaitonStack two-bone IK solver has. It seems way less inclined to freak out now. However, it's unclear if the intention was to solve situations where you might have a bone chain with non-uniform rolls, since that still appears to break the IK and cause mangling. Whatever the case, it does seem to be an improvement over the old implementation at least which was seemed very broken.

However, more importantly, it is worth noting that while this improves two-bone IK, it also appears to have caused regressions in the FABRIK IK solvers, both the newer skeleton modifier stack implementation (which completely freaks out now), and the old SkeletonIK3D node (which seems to behave consistently now regardless of bone rolls but is otherwise mangled. This one may end up being deprecated in the future, but I still don't think it should be knowingly broken). It looks like the other comments did mention FABRIK and how since this PR doesn't touch it directly, I'm guessing the changes to the rotate_to_align function must have caused the regression.

I've attached a sample project which demonstrates comparisons of some IK solvers to demonstrate the issues I described.
[ik_test_2.zip](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/files/8857280/ik_test_2.zip)
edit: fixed example project

author: lawnjelly

body:
> Can the signal not come from the particle system, rather than the particle?

This is already what happens, but the issue is passing the data to the signal in a way that is compatible with the scripting API. GDScript does not have a `struct` type, so type-safe data must be passed via custom objects.

We could emit an array of dictionaries in the signal, but it's not strongly typed. This means there's no autocompletion and errors will only occur at run-time in scripts (instead of compile-time). Parts of the Godot API use dictionaries for that reason, but I feel this is something that should be avoided in the long run.
author: Calinou

body: Making the particle system super inefficient to work around bugs / deficiencies in the scripting seems a case of the tail wagging the dog. Imo it is important the particle system works efficiently, then problems in gdscript can be addressed in further PRs.
